entries.title: 4.1 The Philosophy of Alloy: A Lightweight but Powerful Approach
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: 4.1 The Philosophy of Alloy: A Lightweight but Powerful Approach
entries.text: 4.1 The Philosophy of Alloy: A Lightweight but Powerful Approach

entries.title: The Real Meaning of "Lightweight"
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: The Real Meaning of "Lightweight"
entries.text: The notion of lightweight formal methods was popularized by Daniel Jackson at MIT. The term does not simply mean that the tool is easy to use. It expresses a fundamentally different stance toward formal methods. Traditional heavyweight formal methods aimed at complete proof. The goal was to prove every relevant property mathematically and thereby guarantee correctness completely. That perfectionist approach had a serious weakness. The learning cost was high, adoption required a long lead time, and the methods were rarely used in ordinary projects. Alloy takes a different path. Its lightweight approach prioritizes practical value over completeness. Instead of trying to deliver a full proof, it focuses on discovering counterexamples. Rather than proving that every case is correct, it tries to find concrete situations in which the design fails, and uses those findings to improve the design.

entries.title: A Heuristic Method Driven by Counterexamples
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: A Heuristic Method Driven by Counterexamples
entries.text: At the core of Alloy is a counterexample-driven mindset. The idea is close to scientific hypothesis testing. A scientist cannot usually prove a theory completely, but can improve the theory by finding evidence that contradicts it. Software design works similarly. Proving that a design is perfect is difficult, but finding a concrete example that demonstrates a flaw is often much easier. Alloy automates that discovery process. Suppose you are designing an access-control system and want to ensure that only authorized users can access a file. A traditional approach tries to construct a mathematical proof. In Alloy, we instead ask whether there exists a situation in which an unauthorized user can access the file. If such a situation exists, the design is wrong, and the counterexample gives immediate guidance on how to repair it.

entries.title: Exhaustive Exploration Within a Finite Scope
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Exhaustive Exploration Within a Finite Scope
entries.text: Another key feature of Alloy is exhaustive exploration within a finite scope. Real systems have unbounded possibilities, but many important defects can be discovered through small examples. Alloy therefore explores every possible instance within a specified bound, such as \"up to three users\" or \"up to five files.\" This bound looks like a limitation at first, but in practice it is a major strength. Inside the chosen scope, the analysis is mathematically rigorous. And empirically, many design defects do show up in small instances. For example, when analyzing the possibility of deadlock in a transaction system, two transactions and two resources are often enough to expose the core problem. We do not need hundreds of transactions before the design flaw becomes visible.

entries.title: Supporting Exploratory Design
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Supporting Exploratory Design
entries.text: Alloy is not primarily a proof tool. It is an exploration tool. It gives designers an environment in which they can formulate hypotheses and test them quickly. That makes iterative improvement at the design stage much more practical. In conventional development, design defects are often discovered during implementation or testing, when the cost of repair is already high. Alloy moves many of those discoveries forward into the design phase, where changes are cheaper and the feedback cycle is shorter.

entries.title: Background at MIT and Its Lasting Influence
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Background at MIT and Its Lasting Influence
entries.text: Alloy was developed by the Software Design Group at MIT. Daniel Jackson argued that many software failures are not implementation problems but conceptual design problems. According to this view, the decisive mistakes are often made before any code is written. That insight shaped Alloy. It is specialized for modeling the essential structure and behavior of a system without committing to implementation details. It intentionally stays away from programming-language details, low-level optimization, and concrete algorithms, and instead focuses on high-level design questions such as what the system does and which constraints it must satisfy.

entries.title: Adoption in Industry and Successful Use Cases
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Adoption in Industry and Successful Use Cases
entries.text: Alloy began as an academic tool, but it is now used in practice as well, especially for finding specification holes and consistency violations early by means of bounded checking during design. What Alloy Analyzer provides is bounded exploration within a given scope. The guarantee is therefore only that no counterexample exists within that scope. If you want to claim a more general result, you need an additional argument for why the chosen abstraction and scope are adequate, for example by appealing to the small-scope hypothesis. References (primary or official sources): Alloy Tools (official): Daniel Jackson, \"Alloy: A lightweight object modelling notation\" (2002): Figure 4-1: The overall Alloy approach to lightweight formal methods

entries.title: 4.2 Alloy's Worldview: Relational Modeling
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: 4.2 Alloy's Worldview: Relational Modeling
entries.text: 4.2 Alloy's Worldview: Relational Modeling

entries.title: A Paradigm Shift from Objects to Relations
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: A Paradigm Shift from Objects to Relations
entries.text: Most programmers are familiar with an object-oriented worldview. We think in terms of objects, attributes, methods, and messages exchanged among objects. That worldview is powerful, but it can also limit how we understand system structure. Alloy proposes a different worldview: a relational one. A system is described as a collection of atoms and relations among them. This perspective allows us to see the structure of the system at a higher level of abstraction and, in many cases, more directly. Consider a university enrollment system. In an object-oriented design, we might create Student objects and Course objects, and give a student object an attribute such as an enrollment list. In Alloy, we instead describe the sets Student and Course and the relation enrollment. 【Context-dependent snippet】 In this representation, enrollment is a field of type Student -> Course. For a student s, the expression s.enrollment denotes the set of courses that s is enrolled in. Once the relation is explicit, a wide range of constraints and properties can be described naturally.

entries.title: The Structure of the World as Relations
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: The Structure of the World as Relations
entries.text: In a relational worldview, everything is understood as a relation. This is close to the relational model of databases and to graph-theoretic thinking. Ownership relation: a user owns a file Containment relation: a folder contains a file Dependency relation: one module depends on another Inheritance relation: one class extends another Communication relation: one process communicates with another By modeling these relations explicitly, we can analyze the structural properties of the system in a disciplined way.

entries.title: Atoms and Signatures
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Atoms and Signatures
entries.text: The most basic building blocks in Alloy are atoms. Atoms are indivisible elements that represent the entities of the system. Atoms are grouped by signatures. 【Pseudo notation】 In this example, Person, Student, and Teacher are signatures. The individual elements that belong to those signatures, such as a concrete student or a concrete teacher, are atoms.

entries.title: Fields as Relational Structure
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Fields as Relational Structure
entries.text: The fields of a signature are, in fact, relations. age: Int is a relation from Person to integers, and friends: set Person is a relation from one Person to zero or more other Person atoms. This relational view lets us describe complex structure compactly. 【Pseudo notation】

entries.title: Facts as Constraints
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Facts as Constraints
entries.text: In Alloy, constraints that must hold in every valid model are written as facts. 【Pseudo notation】

entries.title: The Expressive Power of Multiplicity
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: The Expressive Power of Multiplicity
entries.text: Alloy allows multiplicity constraints to be attached to relations. one: exactly one lone: at most one some: at least one set: any number, including zero 【Pseudo notation】

entries.title: Rich Relational Operators
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Rich Relational Operators
entries.text: Alloy provides powerful operators over relations. Join: r.s Transpose: r Transitive closure: ^r Reflexive transitive closure: r Examples: 【Pseudo notation】 The first expression denotes all ancestors except the person itself. The second computes mutual-follow relationships. The third denotes all reachable descendants including the starting directory.

entries.title: Expressing Constraints Relationally
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Expressing Constraints Relationally
entries.text: Once the model is centered on relations, even complicated constraints become natural to write. 【Pseudo notation】

entries.title: 4.3 Describing Basic Structure
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: 4.3 Describing Basic Structure
entries.text: 4.3 Describing Basic Structure

entries.title: A First Alloy Model: An Address Book
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: A First Alloy Model: An Address Book
entries.text: To learn Alloy modeling, let us begin with a familiar example: an address-book system. Even this small example captures the basic syntax and habits of Alloy. 【Context-dependent snippet】 Even this small model already expresses several important points. Every contact has exactly one name. A contact may have no address at all (lone means zero or one). A contact may have multiple friends.

entries.title: Using Signature Hierarchies
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Using Signature Hierarchies
entries.text: To express more elaborate structure, we can exploit inheritance among signatures. 【Context-dependent snippet】 The keyword abstract means that there are no direct atoms of Contact. Only atoms of the concrete extending signatures exist.

entries.title: Refining Structure with Constraints
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Refining Structure with Constraints
entries.text: Bare structure is rarely enough. To make the model realistic, we add facts that capture consistency requirements. 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: Representing Dynamic Behavior
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Representing Dynamic Behavior
entries.text: Alloy can also describe dynamic behavior, not only static structure. One classic style introduces time explicitly and models change across time points. 【Context-dependent snippet】 This style is still useful to understand because older Alloy models and a large body of educational material use explicit Time or State signatures together with util/ordering.

entries.title: Practical Example 1: Access Control in an Email System
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Practical Example 1: Access Control in an Email System
entries.text: Let us move to a more realistic example: access control for an email system. 【Context-dependent snippet】 This example shows how signatures, role-permission assignments, and assertions combine to form a precise security model.

entries.title: Practical Example 2: Inventory Management for an Online Bookstore
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Practical Example 2: Inventory Management for an Online Bookstore
entries.text: The next example includes richer business logic. 【Context-dependent snippet】 This model is still abstract, but it already captures inventory constraints, order consistency, and state changes over time.

entries.title: Practical Example 3: Leader Election in a Distributed System
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Practical Example 3: Leader Election in a Distributed System
entries.text: As a more advanced example, we can model a leader-election algorithm in a distributed system. 【Context-dependent snippet】 For a self-contained executable example, see examples/ch04/leader-election.als. These practical examples connect directly to later chapters, especially Chapter 8 on model checking and Chapter 11 on integrating formal methods into the development process.

entries.title: Relationships Across Multiple Signatures
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Relationships Across Multiple Signatures
entries.text: Real systems contain multiple kinds of entities connected in nontrivial ways. 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: Defining Utility Predicates
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Defining Utility Predicates
entries.text: As models grow, reusable predicates improve readability and reduce duplication. 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: Building a Model Incrementally
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Building a Model Incrementally
entries.text: Large models should be built in stages. Stage 1: basic entities and relations 【Context-dependent snippet】 Stage 2: add constraints 【Context-dependent snippet】 Stage 3: introduce richer relations 【Context-dependent snippet】 Stage 4: refine policy and consistency requirements 【Pseudo notation】 Because the final stage contains ellipsis, it is explanatory pseudo notation rather than code that can be pasted directly into the tool.

entries.title: 4.4 Expressing Constraints and Properties
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: 4.4 Expressing Constraints and Properties
entries.text: 4.4 Expressing Constraints and Properties

entries.title: Constraints as Logical Formulas
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Constraints as Logical Formulas
entries.text: One of Alloy's main strengths is that complex constraints can be described as logical formulas. Requirements that would remain ambiguous in natural language can be expressed precisely. The basic logical operators are: and or &&: conjunction or or · · : disjunction not or !: negation implies or =>: implication iff or : equivalence Example: constraints for a university enrollment system. 【Context-dependent snippet】 For a self-contained model, see examples/ch04/university-enrollment.als.

entries.title: General Constraints with Quantifiers
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: General Constraints with Quantifiers
entries.text: Quantifiers express notions such as \"for all\" and \"there exists.\" all x: Set · formula some x: Set · formula no x: Set · formula one x: Set · formula lone x: Set · formula Example: constraints for a file system. 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: Expressing Relations with Set Operators
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Expressing Relations with Set Operators
entries.text: Alloy also relies heavily on set operators. s1 + s2: union s1 & s2: intersection s1 - s2: difference s in t: subset relation #s: cardinality Banking example: 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: Expressing Temporal Properties
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Expressing Temporal Properties
entries.text: Alloy can also express dynamic behavior and time-dependent requirements. 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: Formalizing Security Policies
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Formalizing Security Policies
entries.text: Alloy is particularly well suited to the description of security policies. 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: Layering Invariants
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Layering Invariants
entries.text: In a complex system, constraints should be organized in layers. 【Context-dependent snippet】 This layering clarifies the purpose and criticality of the constraints and supports staged verification.

entries.title: 4.5 Practical Verification with Alloy Analyzer
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: 4.5 Practical Verification with Alloy Analyzer
entries.text: 4.5 Practical Verification with Alloy Analyzer

entries.title: The Basic Idea of Model Checking
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: The Basic Idea of Model Checking
entries.text: Alloy Analyzer is the tool that actually explores the model. It applies model checking within the chosen scope, searching all possible instances in that bounded space and reporting constraint violations or unexpected behavior. The basic workflow is: Generate instances: produce structures that satisfy the facts. Check properties: test whether a target property holds. Find counterexamples: if the property fails, produce a concrete failing instance. Analyze results: use the result to improve the model.

entries.title: First Check: Generating Instances
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: First Check: Generating Instances
entries.text: The first step is often to confirm that the model has meaningful instances. Using the address-book example: 【Context-dependent snippet】 The command run checks whether an instance satisfying the constraints exists. The scope for 3 means that the exploration may use at most three atoms of the relevant signatures.

entries.title: Assertions: Checking Properties
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Assertions: Checking Properties
entries.text: The keyword assert lets us state properties that the model is expected to satisfy. 【Context-dependent snippet】 The command check looks for a counterexample to the assertion. If one is found, the analyzer shows a concrete instance.

entries.title: Interpreting and Using Counterexamples
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Interpreting and Using Counterexamples
entries.text: Consider the following model: 【Context-dependent snippet】 The assertion NoFileSharing fails. A counterexample may look as follows. 【Pseudo notation】 This counterexample makes the design contradiction explicit: the model of group sharing allows multiple users to hold the same file, which conflicts with the assertion.

entries.title: Choosing Scopes Effectively
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Choosing Scopes Effectively
entries.text: The chosen scope has a major effect on both performance and usefulness. 【Context-dependent snippet】 General guidelines: Start with a small scope, such as two or three elements, to find basic problems quickly. If no issue is found, increase the scope gradually. If one kind of element is especially important, control that scope separately.

entries.title: Staged Verification with Predicates
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Staged Verification with Predicates
entries.text: Complex scenarios can be explored in stages by using predicates. 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: The Iterative Model-Improvement Process
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: The Iterative Model-Improvement Process
entries.text: Practical use of Alloy usually follows an iterative cycle. Build the initial model 【Context-dependent snippet】 Perform a first verification 【Context-dependent snippet】 Discover a problem and refine requirements 【Context-dependent snippet】 Add a new constraint 【Context-dependent snippet】 Verify again 【Context-dependent snippet】 By repeating this cycle, the model can be improved incrementally instead of being treated as fixed from the beginning.

entries.title: 4.6 Alloy 6 Extensions: Mutable State and Temporal Logic
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: 4.6 Alloy 6 Extensions: Mutable State and Temporal Logic
entries.text: Historically, Alloy was strongest for checking the consistency of static structure expressed as relations. In Alloy 6, however, the introduction of var and temporal operators makes it possible to describe state transitions and traces directly. This reduces the need for the classic State-signature plus ordering encoding that was common in Alloy 4. The execution environment used in this book, described in Appendix B, fixes Alloy 6.2.0 through tools/bootstrap.sh. The same commands should therefore be reproducible for readers unless they intentionally override ALLOYVERSION.

entries.title: Basic Syntax in Alloy 6
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Basic Syntax in Alloy 6
entries.text: var: declare a signature or field whose value changes across states ' (prime): refer to the value in the next state, as in Trash' = Trash + f temporal operators such as always, eventually, once, and after n steps: bound the trace length; larger scope and more steps mean higher exploration cost

entries.title: Example: The Trash Model
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Example: The Trash Model
entries.text: examples/alloy/trash-temporal.als is a minimal model with a set File and a mutable subset Trash. Delete and restore operations are described as transitions, and a simple safety property is checked. 【Context-dependent snippet】 Command-line execution: 【Context-dependent snippet】 How to read the results: run checks for the existence of a trace. SAT means that a trace satisfying the condition exists. check looks for a counterexample. UNSAT means that no counterexample was found within the given scope and step bound. It does not mean that a general proof has been established outside those bounds. Generated artifacts: The trace is written to .artifacts/alloy/trash-temporal/example-solution-0.md. To visualize it in the GUI, run java -jar tools/.cache/alloy-6.2.0.jar gui and step through the states interactively.

entries.title: Executable Example Contracts
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Executable Example Contracts
entries.text: The Alloy fragments in this chapter are context-dependent snippets that must be read together with the surrounding explanation, declarations, and scope choices. CI-backed executability is limited to the contract blocks below, which call the shared JA/EN canonical assets under examples/ through the manifest runner. Repository-wide, pr-quick and nightly are separated, and the Alloy contracts in this chapter define the pr-quick baseline. Normalized execution evidence is expected under .artifacts/manifest//, and readers should retrieve the canonical files from the same repository revision instead of copying inline snippets in isolation. alloy-collection: canonical asset examples/alloy/collection.als; lane pr-quick; pinned version Alloy 6.2.0; expected success marker Alloy exec: examples/alloy/collection.als; run$1-solution-0.md is also generated as an execution artifact. Retrieve examples/alloy/collection.als from the same commit as the manuscript. 【Tool-compliant (runs as-is)】 alloy-university-enrollment: canonical asset examples/ch04/university-enrollment.als; lane pr-quick; pinned version Alloy 6.2.0; expected success marker Alloy exec: examples/ch04/university-enrollment.als; run$1-solution-0.md is also generated as an execution artifact. Retrieve the self-contained file from examples/ch04/university-enrollment.als in the same revision. 【Tool-compliant (runs as-is)】 alloy-leader-election: canonical asset examples/ch04/leader-election.als; lane pr-quick; pinned version Alloy 6.2.0; expected success marker Alloy exec: examples/ch04/leader-election.als; the three solution files are also generated as execution artifacts. Retrieve the shared JA/EN source file from examples/ch04/leader-election.als. 【Tool-compliant (runs as-is)】 alloy-trash-temporal: canonical asset examples/alloy/trash-temporal.als; lane pr-quick; pinned version Alloy 6.2.0; expected success marker Alloy exec: examples/alloy/trash-temporal.als; example-solution-0.md is also generated as an execution artifact. Retrieve examples/alloy/trash-temporal.als from the same repository revision rather than copying the inline excerpt alone. 【Tool-compliant (runs as-is)】

entries.title: 4.7 Learning from Counterexamples: The Design Improvement Cycle
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: 4.7 Learning from Counterexamples: The Design Improvement Cycle
entries.text: 4.7 Learning from Counterexamples: The Design Improvement Cycle

entries.title: The Educational Value of Counterexamples
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: The Educational Value of Counterexamples
entries.text: The counterexamples produced by Alloy Analyzer are more than error messages. They are concrete learning material. A counterexample shows not only that a problem exists, but also how the design decisions made that problem possible. Strong designers do not stop at \"there is a bug.\" They ask deeper questions: Why is this situation possible? Is it truly unacceptable? Is the root cause a missing constraint, a vague requirement, or a flawed design concept?

entries.title: Example: Improving an Access-Control System
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Example: Improving an Access-Control System
entries.text: Let us trace an improvement cycle for a file access-control system. Initial design: 【Context-dependent snippet】 Counterexample: 【Pseudo notation】 The counterexample shows that User0 can read File1. That may be contrary to the designer's intent, but under the current facts it is legal. Design review prompted by the counterexample: Should file sharing be allowed at all? If it is allowed, what control mechanism is required? How should read permission for non-owners be represented? Improved design: 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: Extending the Design to Group-Based Access Control
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Extending the Design to Group-Based Access Control
entries.text: Even this simple sharing model may reveal further counterexamples. 【Context-dependent snippet】 This exploration shows that a single file may be shared with many users. That may or may not be acceptable, depending on the requirements. If it becomes a management problem, a group-based model may be more appropriate. Improved group-based model: 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: Finding and Addressing Temporal Constraints
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Finding and Addressing Temporal Constraints
entries.text: Counterexamples can also reveal temporal design problems. 【Context-dependent snippet】 This exploration reveals the possibility of concurrent sessions for the same user. Whether that is acceptable depends on the intended policy.

entries.title: Predicting Performance Problems
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Predicting Performance Problems
entries.text: Alloy can also help predict performance risks. 【Context-dependent snippet】 This model does not measure runtime directly, but it does expose structures that are likely to produce poor performance.

entries.title: A Methodology for Counterexample-Driven Design Improvement
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: A Methodology for Counterexample-Driven Design Improvement
entries.text: To use counterexamples effectively, a systematic process is helpful. Classify the counterexample a genuine design defect an ambiguity in the requirements an incompleteness in the model an unexpected but acceptable situation Analyze the root cause Why does the situation arise? Which constraint is missing? Which assumption was left implicit? Choose a response strategy add a constraint restructure the model clarify the requirement accept the behavior if it is not actually problematic Verify the improvement confirm the effect of the new constraint evaluate its impact on other properties rerun the analysis at a larger scope if needed With this disciplined approach, counterexamples become one of the most effective tools for improving a design.

entries.title: End-of-Chapter Exercises
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: End-of-Chapter Exercises
entries.text: If you use AI while working through these exercises Treat AI output as a proposal; use verifiers to make the final judgment. Keep a record of the prompts you used, the generated specifications and invariants, the verification commands and logs (including seed, depth, and scope), and the revision history when counterexamples were found. For detailed templates, see Appendix D and Appendix F.

entries.title: Basic Exercise 1: Reading an Alloy Model
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Basic Exercise 1: Reading an Alloy Model
entries.text: Read the following Alloy model and explain the system structure and constraints that it represents. 【Context-dependent snippet】 Explain: The real-world concepts represented by the signatures The meaning and plausibility of each constraint Whether there are family relationships that the model cannot represent Plausible counterexamples and what they would mean

entries.title: Basic Exercise 2: Writing Constraints
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Basic Exercise 2: Writing Constraints
entries.text: For an online library system, write Alloy constraints satisfying the following requirements. Requirements: A user may borrow multiple books. A book may not be lent to multiple people at the same time. A user may borrow at most five books. A user with overdue items may not borrow new books. Every book has a due date. Define the necessary signatures and facts.

entries.title: Practical Exercise 1: Modeling a Security Policy
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Practical Exercise 1: Modeling a Security Policy
entries.text: Model a file access-control system in Alloy that satisfies the following requirements. Functional requirements: Users can own files. Files are either read-only or read-write. Groups can be created and users can be added to them. Files can be shared with groups. Security requirements: The owner can always access the file. Group members can access only files shared with that group. Writing to a read-only file is forbidden. Administrators can access all files. Tasks: Define appropriate signatures. Write facts for the constraints. Write assertions for the security properties. Verify them using check.

entries.title: Practical Exercise 2: Model Checking and Improvement
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Practical Exercise 2: Model Checking and Improvement
entries.text: For the model created in the previous exercise: Try various run commands to generate instances. Execute checks under different scopes. Analyze any counterexamples that are found. Improve the constraints or the design. Verify the improved version again.

entries.title: Advanced Exercise: Modeling Dynamic Behavior
entries.chapter: Chapter 4: Introduction to Lightweight Formal Methods — Specification with Alloy
entries.heading: Advanced Exercise: Modeling Dynamic Behavior
entries.text: Using Alloy 6 var declarations and temporal operators, model the dynamic behavior of the following ATM system, referring to Section 4.6. ATM system: Accounts have balances. A withdrawal decreases the balance. A deposit increases the balance. A withdrawal is forbidden if funds are insufficient. Every transaction is recorded in the history. Tasks: Declare state such as account balances and transaction history using var. Define each operation as a current-state to next-state predicate using expressions such as x' = .... Write invariants and forbidden conditions with always, and search for counterexamples with check. Adjust both the exploration scope and the trace length with for ... but ... steps, then iterate through counterexample, repair, and re-verification. Supplement: a classic Alloy 4 style based on an explicit State signature and util/ordering can still be used, but this book treats the Alloy 6 notation as the primary style. Properties to verify: Balances remain non-negative. The total amount of money is preserved before and after each transaction. Every transaction is recorded in the history. By working through these exercises, you should develop practical skill in Alloy-based modeling and be prepared for the more advanced formal methods presented in the next chapter.

entries.title: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.text: Translation status: Partial. Reviewed against Japanese source commit 83d031100ae7 on 2026-07-16. Some content, headings, examples, tables, or references remain partially synchronized. Track the remaining work.

entries.title: 5.1 The World of Z Notation: Thinking in Terms of State and Schemas
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: 5.1 The World of Z Notation: Thinking in Terms of State and Schemas
entries.text: 5.1 The World of Z Notation: Thinking in Terms of State and Schemas

entries.title: A Culture of Precision Born in Oxford
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: A Culture of Precision Born in Oxford
entries.text: Z notation was developed at the Oxford University Computing Laboratory from the late 1970s into the 1980s. The background was a growing recognition that software systems were becoming too complex for informal methods to guarantee quality reliably. The creators of Z tried to address this problem by importing mathematical rigor into software specification. Their goal, however, was not merely to write software documents with mathematical symbols. They wanted a practical form of mathematical rigor that software engineers could actually use. The result was Z notation, which is often described as "readable mathematics." Complex mathematical ideas are expressed in a structured way, making them usable not only by mathematicians but also by practitioners who need to design and review software systems.

entries.title: A State-Centered Worldview
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: A State-Centered Worldview
entries.text: The defining feature of Z notation is its state-centered worldview. A system is understood as a combination of state and operations that transform that state. This viewpoint is close to database design and state-machine thinking. When we think in state-centered terms, we first identify the information the system must carry, namely its state variables. Next, we define invariants over those variables. Only then do we describe the operations that change the state. That order matters. It is impossible to discuss whether an operation is correct unless the valid states of the system are already understood. Consider a library system. A conventional object-oriented approach may start from a Book object or a Member object. Z begins elsewhere: what is the state of the library? Which books exist, which are on loan, and who has borrowed which book? Those pieces of information and their relations define the system state.

entries.title: The Structured Wisdom of Schemas
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: The Structured Wisdom of Schemas
entries.text: One of the most innovative ideas in Z notation is the schema. A schema is a way to package related declarations and constraints together. This is not just a notational convenience. It is a deep mechanism for managing complexity. Figure 5-1: Schema structure in Z notation and state-based specification Human cognitive capacity is limited. A software system may contain dozens or hundreds of relevant elements, but a person cannot reason about all of them at once. Schemas are an abstraction mechanism for overcoming that limit. By grouping related variables and constraints into one conceptually coherent unit, a schema lets us break a large system into understandable pieces. Schemas are also reusable, so recurring structures can be captured once and then included elsewhere.

entries.title: Putting Mathematical Foundations to Work
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Putting Mathematical Foundations to Work
entries.text: Z notation is built on established mathematical foundations such as set theory, logic, and functions. It does not simply reproduce those subjects verbatim. It adapts them into a practical notation for software specification. For example, the mathematical idea of a function is refined into several categories that matter directly in software: partial functions, total functions, injections, and so on. Each of these captures a different aspect of system structure or behavior. Z also places strong emphasis on types. This is reminiscent of type systems in programming languages, but Z is often more expressive. Types help detect mistakes early and make a specification easier to understand.

entries.title: Industrial Successes and Lessons Learned
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Industrial Successes and Lessons Learned
entries.text: Z notation has a long record not only in academic work but also in industry, especially in safety-critical domains such as transportation control, aerospace systems, and nuclear applications. One major lesson from these cases is the importance of gradual adoption. It is usually ineffective to rewrite an entire system specification in Z at once. A better approach is to begin with the most critical part of the system and then expand the scope step by step. Another lesson is that Z succeeds only when a team develops a shared understanding of how to use it. Learning the notation is necessary, but not sufficient. The team also has to build a culture in which the notation supports review and communication.

entries.title: 5.2 The System of Mathematical Notation: Making Sets and Logic Practical
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: 5.2 The System of Mathematical Notation: Making Sets and Logic Practical
entries.text: 5.2 The System of Mathematical Notation: Making Sets and Logic Practical

entries.title: Symbols as a Common Language
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Symbols as a Common Language
entries.text: The greatest value of mathematical notation is uniqueness of interpretation. Natural language makes expressions such as "large," "small," "many," or "few" ambiguous. Symbols such as ≤, ≥, ∈, and ⊆ have stable meanings independent of language and region. Z notation builds a software-oriented notation on top of that stability. The important thing is not memorizing symbols mechanically, but understanding the concept each symbol captures.

entries.title: Basic Types and Constructed Types
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Basic Types and Constructed Types
entries.text: In Z, we begin by defining basic types and then combine them to construct more elaborate types. Examples of basic types: 【Context-dependent snippet】 Names in square brackets denote basic types. Each one represents a set of primitive elements that we do not decompose any further. Examples of constructed types: 【Context-dependent snippet】 Constructors such as sequences, free types, and record types allow us to build new types from simpler ones.

entries.title: Practical Use of Set Operations
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Practical Use of Set Operations
entries.text: Set-theoretic operators are natural and powerful in software specification. Basic set operators: ∈ (membership) ⊆ (subset) ∪ (union) ∩ (intersection) \ (difference) ℙ (power set) Practical example: library stock management 【Context-dependent snippet】 Such expressions are far more precise than natural language, and often more readable than code.

entries.title: The Hierarchy of Relations and Functions
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: The Hierarchy of Relations and Functions
entries.text: Z notation distinguishes clearly between general relations and the various special cases of functions. Basic relation and function symbols used in this chapter X ↔ Y: an arbitrary binary relation X ⇸ Y: a partial function X → Y: a total function X ↣ Y: an injection x ↦ y: a maplet, that is, an explicit pair Practical example: student enrollment 【擬似記法】 The enrollment relation is many-to-many. An advisor is modeled as a partial function because some students may not yet have an advisor. teaches is a total function to sets of courses, and studentID is injective because student numbers must be unique.

entries.title: Practical Use of Predicate Logic
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Practical Use of Predicate Logic
entries.text: Quantifiers such as ∀ and ∃ are central in specification writing. Universal quantification: 【擬似記法】 The first formula states that every student is under the enrollment limit. The second states that every borrowed book has a due date at least fourteen days after its borrowing date. Existential quantification: 【擬似記法】 The first says that at least one active administrator exists. The second says that exactly one person has the target email address.

entries.title: Integrating Notation Inside a Schema
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Integrating Notation Inside a Schema
entries.text: The real strength of Z appears when these mathematical building blocks are combined inside a schema. 【擬似記法】 This schema gives a mathematically precise description of library state, while remaining readable at the design level.

entries.title: Reading and Pronouncing the Notation
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Reading and Pronouncing the Notation
entries.text: To use Z effectively in practice, a team must be able to read the symbols correctly. This matters for discussion and review. Typical readings: x ∈ S: "x belongs to S" or "x is in S" A ⊆ B: "A is a subset of B" f: X → Y: "f is a function from X to Y" ∀ x: X • P(x): "for all x in X, P of x" ∃ x: X • P(x): "there exists x in X such that P of x" Once the team shares a consistent way of reading the notation, Z becomes much more practical as a communication medium.

entries.title: 5.3 Describing State with Schemas
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: 5.3 Describing State with Schemas
entries.text: 5.3 Describing State with Schemas

entries.title: The Fundamental Idea of Structured State
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: The Fundamental Idea of Structured State
entries.text: To understand the state of a complex system, some kind of structure is essential. Schemas in Z provide that structure. They do more than group variables. They also collect the constraints that maintain consistency. This makes it possible to answer a fundamental design question precisely: what counts as a valid state of the system?

entries.title: The Basic Structure of a Schema
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: The Basic Structure of a Schema
entries.text: A Z schema consists of a declaration part and a predicate part. The declaration part introduces state variables and their types. The predicate part states the relationships and constraints among them. 【擬似記法】 The upper part is the declaration part. The lower part contains invariants that must always hold.

entries.title: Representing Composite State
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Representing Composite State
entries.text: Real systems usually contain several interrelated collections and mappings. Schemas can express those compound structures naturally. 【擬似記法】 This schema captures a realistic library state. Each constraint represents a piece of the system's integrity policy.

entries.title: Hierarchical Schema Design
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Hierarchical Schema Design
entries.text: Large systems benefit from hierarchical structuring. Lower-level schemas can be combined into higher-level ones. 【擬似記法】 Hierarchical organization preserves local clarity while controlling overall complexity.

entries.title: Expressing State Invariants
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Expressing State Invariants
entries.text: State invariants are among the most important elements of a Z specification. They capture the consistency properties that must survive every operation. 【擬似記法】 These invariants encode the core of the business logic, such as inventory consistency and order validity.

entries.title: Expressing Conditional State
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Expressing Conditional State
entries.text: System constraints often depend on conditions. Z can express such conditional state restrictions naturally. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Considering Time-Dependent State
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Considering Time-Dependent State
entries.text: In dynamic systems, state evolves over time. Z can model that dependence explicitly when needed. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Designing for Readability
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Designing for Readability
entries.text: Readable schemas are easier to review and maintain. The order of declarations, the grouping of related constraints, and naming discipline all matter. Useful principles include: Place related variables together. Write constraints in a logical order. Use meaningful names. Split complex constraints over multiple lines. 【擬似記法】 Structured presentation makes the intention of the schema visible, not just the raw formulas.

entries.title: 5.4 Operation Schemas: Describing State Change
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: 5.4 Operation Schemas: Describing State Change
entries.text: 5.4 Operation Schemas: Describing State Change

entries.title: Operations as State Transformations
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Operations as State Transformations
entries.text: In Z notation, an operation is understood as a state transformation. It defines the mathematical relation between a pre-state and a post-state. That viewpoint makes it possible to reason rigorously about correctness and about how operations interact. An operation schema describes the current state, the next state, any inputs, and any outputs. It makes explicit not only what the operation changes, but also what it leaves unchanged.

entries.title: Delta Schemas and Xi Schemas
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Delta Schemas and Xi Schemas
entries.text: Z provides dedicated notation for operations. Delta notation (Δ): the operation changes state. 【擬似記法】 This means that both the pre-state Library and the post-state Library' are in scope. Xi notation (Ξ): the operation does not change state. 【擬似記法】 It states that all bindings in the schema are preserved across the operation.

entries.title: A Detailed Borrow Operation
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: A Detailed Borrow Operation
entries.text: Let us examine a book-borrowing operation in a library system. 【擬似記法】 Input variables are marked with ? and outputs with !. The schema states both the conditions required for normal execution and the resulting next state.

entries.title: Formalizing Error Handling
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Formalizing Error Handling
entries.text: Real systems must define error behavior as carefully as success behavior. Z can capture both in the same overall framework. 【擬似記法】 Because the schema uses ΞLibrary, the state remains unchanged in the error case.

entries.title: A Complete Operation Definition
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: A Complete Operation Definition
entries.text: A complete operation combines the normal and error cases. 【擬似記法】 This definition ensures that the operation has specified behavior for every relevant input category.

entries.title: Structuring More Complex Operations
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Structuring More Complex Operations
entries.text: For more elaborate business logic, it is often helpful to describe the operation in stages. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Before-and-After Relationships
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Before-and-After Relationships
entries.text: Operation schemas are especially valuable because they let us state exactly how the pre-state and post-state are related. 【擬似記法】 The explicit unchanged part is the response to the frame problem in this style of specification.

entries.title: Composition and Order of Operations
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Composition and Order of Operations
entries.text: When multiple operations are combined, their order and dependency have to be made explicit. 【Context-dependent snippet】 The operator ⨾ denotes sequential composition.

entries.title: Conditional Operations
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Conditional Operations
entries.text: Operations can also branch based on conditions. 【擬似記法】 This one schema covers both the on-time return case and the overdue case.

entries.title: The Algebraic Structure of Operations
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: The Algebraic Structure of Operations
entries.text: The deeper power of Z notation lies in its ability to compose simple operations into more complex ones. This is analogous to algebra, where operators can be combined to derive larger expressions. That algebraic view improves reuse, supports layered design, and makes relationships among operations mathematically analyzable.

entries.title: Schema Composition
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Schema Composition
entries.text: The most basic schema-calculus operation is composition through logical operators. Composition by conjunction (∧): 【擬似記法】 This defines an operation that must satisfy both the authentication constraint and the system-operation constraint. Composition by disjunction (∨): 【擬似記法】 This schema allows one of several payment mechanisms.

entries.title: Sequential Composition

entries.heading: Sequential Composition
entries.text: Many real operations consist of several stages executed in sequence. 【Context-dependent snippet】 Each stage feeds into the next one, together forming a larger transaction.

entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Expressing Conditional Branches
entries.text: Conditional behavior can also be written in schema-calculus style. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Integrating Error Handling
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Integrating Error Handling
entries.text: Production systems need a clean integration of success and failure behavior. 【擬似記法】 A more concrete example: 【擬似記法】

entries.heading: Parallel Composition

entries.title: Abstraction and Concretization
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Abstraction and Concretization
entries.text: Schema calculus also supports a stepwise refinement from abstract to concrete operations. Abstract level: 【Context-dependent snippet】 More concrete level: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Minimizing State Change
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Minimizing State Change
entries.text: One response to the frame problem is to define the changed and unchanged parts of the state explicitly. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Reversibility of Operations
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Reversibility of Operations
entries.text: In some domains, inverse operations such as undo are an essential design concern. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Integrating Access Control
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Integrating Access Control
entries.text: Systems with security requirements often need an access-control wrapper around all operations. 【擬似記法】 This generic pattern adds security control to an arbitrary operation X. 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: Performance Characteristics of Operations
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Performance Characteristics of Operations
entries.text: Schema calculus can also help document performance-related design choices. 【擬似記法】 This kind of description does not replace measurement, but it makes the assumed performance strategy explicit in the specification.

entries.title: 5.6 Applying the Method to the Real World: An Elevator Control System
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: 5.6 Applying the Method to the Real World: An Elevator Control System
entries.text: 5.6 Applying the Method to the Real World: An Elevator Control System

entries.title: Analyzing the System Requirements
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Analyzing the System Requirements
entries.text: The elevator control system is a classic example for formal methods. It is safety-critical, contains real-time and concurrent aspects, and exhibits nontrivial state transitions. That makes it a strong example for Z notation. Let us first summarize the main requirements. Functional requirements: respond to passenger calls; determine an efficient movement schedule; stop at requested floors; and control door opening and closing. Safety requirements: the elevator must not move while the doors are open; the cabin must not exceed capacity; and under mechanical failure, the system must stop in a safe condition. Performance requirements: minimize average waiting time; and optimize energy efficiency.

entries.title: The Basic State Model
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: The Basic State Model
entries.text: We can model the system state in stages. Basic type definitions: 【Context-dependent snippet】 Basic state schema: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: The Call-Management Subsystem

entries.heading: The Call-Management Subsystem
entries.text: Handling hall calls and cabin calls is one of the central functions. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Integrated System State

entries.heading: Integrated System State

entries.heading: A More Complex Operation: Scheduling
entries.text: The scheduler is the more intelligent part of the system. It decides the next target floor based on current requests and current direction. 【擬似記法】

entries.heading: A Safety-Critical Operation: Door Control
entries.text: Door control directly affects safety, so the preconditions are especially important. 【擬似記法】

entries.heading: Emergency Operations
entries.text: In a safety-critical system, emergency behavior must also be specified explicitly. 【擬似記法】

entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Verifying System-Level Properties
entries.text: Once the model is complete enough, we can state important system properties. Safety property: 【擬似記法】 Liveness property: 【擬似記法】 The liveness property expresses that if there are pending calls, eventually the set of pending calls becomes smaller.

entries.title: Formalizing Performance Characteristics
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Formalizing Performance Characteristics
entries.text: Formal specification can also record performance expectations. 【擬似記法】 This elevator example illustrates the main value of Z notation: safety, functionality, and performance expectations can all be described in one unified framework.

entries.title: Basic Exercise 1: Reading and Analyzing a Schema
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Basic Exercise 1: Reading and Analyzing a Schema
entries.text: Read the following Z schema and explain the system structure and constraints it represents. 【擬似記法】 Explain: The meaning and type of each variable The meaning and plausibility of each constraint Examples of situations that this model can express Requirements that this model might still fail to express

entries.title: Basic Exercise 2: Writing an Operation Schema
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Basic Exercise 2: Writing an Operation Schema
entries.text: For the university system above, create an operation schema for student course registration. Student registration operation A student registers for a new course. Preconditions: the student exists, the course exists, and the student is not already enrolled. Postcondition: the enrollment relation is extended. Error cases: unknown student, unknown course, duplicate enrollment. Produce: the normal-case operation schema; the error-case operation schema; and the complete operation definition combining both.

entries.title: Practical Exercise 1: Modeling a Banking System
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Practical Exercise 1: Modeling a Banking System
entries.text: Model a banking system in Z notation that satisfies the following requirements. Basic elements: customers (name, address, phone number); accounts (account number, balance, type, owner); and transactions (transaction ID, account, amount, timestamp, type). Constraints: account balances are non-negative; every account has an owner; transactions apply only to existing accounts; and account numbers are unique. Operations: opening an account; depositing money; withdrawing money; and transferring funds between accounts. Tasks: define the state schema; define the operation schema for each operation; and identify the key invariants.

entries.title: Practical Exercise 2: Using Schema Calculus
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Practical Exercise 2: Using Schema Calculus
entries.text: For the banking system from the previous exercise, use schema calculus to achieve the following goals. Define secure operations add authentication checks to every operation; automate logging. Integrate error handling combine normal and error cases consistently; define a uniform error response style. Represent transaction atomicity express transfer as a composition of withdrawal and deposit; handle rollback when an error occurs midway.

entries.title: Advanced Exercise: Specifying a Real-Time System
entries.chapter: Chapter 5: State-Based Specification — Fundamentals of Z Notation
entries.heading: Advanced Exercise: Specifying a Real-Time System
entries.text: Model a traffic-signal control system with a notion of time using Z notation. Requirements: a four-way intersection (north-south and east-west); vehicle signals and pedestrian signals for each direction; safety: conflicting directions must not be green at the same time; efficiency: minimum green-time and maximum red-time constraints; and pedestrian requests through push buttons. Elements to model: signal states (red, yellow, green); time concepts (timers and current time); vehicle-detection sensors; pedestrian request buttons; and signal-change operations. Properties to verify: Safety: unsafe signal combinations never occur. Liveness: every direction eventually receives a green signal. Fairness: no direction waits indefinitely. Through these exercises, you should acquire practical skill in system modeling with Z notation and be ready for the process-centered descriptions in the next chapter.

Unicode symbols such as →, □, and ⊓, along with some guards, where clauses, and event parameter conventions, do not necessarily match CSPM exactly and should not be pasted into a tool unchanged

entries.title: 6.1 The Essence of Concurrency: Why It Is Hard
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: 6.1 The Essence of Concurrency: Why It Is Hard
entries.text: 6.1 The Essence of Concurrency: Why It Is Hard

entries.title: The Limits of Sequential Thinking
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Limits of Sequential Thinking
entries.text: The human brain is basically adapted to sequential reasoning. We are good at thinking about one thing after another and advancing a line of logic step by step. That pattern works very well for single-process programs. A flow such as "do A, then B, and based on the result do C or D" is easy to understand. Concurrent systems are different. Multiple events progress at the same time. In a restaurant, several chefs may be cooking different dishes in parallel while several waiters are serving different tables. Understanding that web of interaction through purely sequential reasoning is difficult. The core difficulty of concurrency is combinatorial explosion in the possible execution patterns. If two processes each perform three operations, there may already be twenty possible interleavings. As the number of processes and the number of operations per process grow, that number increases rapidly.

entries.title: Emergent Complexity Caused by Interaction
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Emergent Complexity Caused by Interaction
entries.text: One of the most interesting and difficult features of concurrent systems is that each individual process may be simple while the interaction among them creates unexpected complexity. This is similar to many-body problems in physics or collective behavior in biology. Consider two processes trying to access the same resource. Each process may be perfectly correct in isolation, yet simultaneous execution can lead to a race condition and produce an unexpected result. 【擬似記法】 If these actions interleave in the wrong way, one update may overwrite the other, causing inconsistent data.

entries.title: Nondeterminism as Reality
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Nondeterminism as Reality
entries.text: In concurrent systems, nondeterminism is unavoidable. The same initial state may lead to different results depending on scheduling and timing. This makes behavior harder to predict and debugging harder to perform. Nondeterminism is not purely negative. It can also support flexibility and performance. The real design challenge is to control it while still ensuring safety and liveness. Figure 6-1: Basic concepts of concurrent systems and communication models in CSP

entries.title: The Growing Complexity of Time and Causality
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Growing Complexity of Time and Causality
entries.text: In a sequential system, temporal order and causality are relatively clear. If A happens before B, then A can be a cause of B. In a concurrent system, physical time and logical time become less aligned, and causal relations become harder to reason about. The problem becomes even more serious in distributed systems. Clock synchronization across nodes is difficult, and even the notion of "simultaneous" becomes slippery. In much the same way that relativity complicated the notion of absolute time in physics, distributed systems force us to reason without a single universal clock.

entries.title: Partial Failure
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Partial Failure
entries.text: In concurrent and distributed systems, one part of the system may fail while the rest continues to operate. This is called partial failure, and it introduces another layer of design complexity. In a conventional sequential system, failure is often global: the whole program crashes. In a distributed system, by contrast, part of the network may be cut off while the remaining nodes continue to run. Maintaining consistency in such a situation is a major design challenge.

entries.title: The Benefits of Concurrency
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Benefits of Concurrency
entries.text: Despite these difficulties, concurrency is indispensable in modern systems. There are clear reasons for that. Higher performance: multiple CPU cores can be used effectively. Better responsiveness: background work can proceed without blocking the user interface. Scalability: capacity can be expanded horizontally. Availability: the system can tolerate some local failures. Natural modeling: many real-world activities are inherently concurrent. Designing concurrent systems is difficult, but with the right abstraction and formal methods, their complexity can be managed. CSP is one of the strongest tools for doing so.

entries.title: 6.2 The CSP Worldview: Processes and Communication
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: 6.2 The CSP Worldview: Processes and Communication
entries.text: 6.2 The CSP Worldview: Processes and Communication

entries.title: Tony Hoare's Insight
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Tony Hoare's Insight
entries.text: CSP, or Communicating Sequential Processes, was introduced by Tony Hoare in Hoare's central insight was that the essence of concurrency lies in communication. The overall behavior of the system depends on how processes exchange messages and synchronize. This was a major shift in perspective. Earlier approaches to concurrency often focused on shared memory and shared variables. Hoare argued that explicit message passing provides a better abstraction for understanding and designing concurrent systems.

entries.title: The Abstraction of a Process
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Abstraction of a Process
entries.text: In CSP, a process is a sequentially executed pattern of behavior. The important point is that the inside of a process can still be understood in the familiar way: step by step. The complexity is concentrated in the interactions among processes. That separation allows us to reason independently about the correctness of a single process and the correctness of process interaction. This is a crucial principle for managing complexity. 【擬似記法】 This expression says that a vending-machine process accepts a coin, then allows the choice of tea or coffee, and finally returns to its original state.

entries.title: Interaction Through Events
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Interaction Through Events
entries.text: In CSP, interaction among processes is abstracted as events. An event may represent message exchange, synchronization, access to a resource, or some other interaction. CSP treats them in one uniform way. The crucial property of an event is synchrony. An event occurs only when all processes involved in that event are ready. That makes coordination explicit. 【擬似記法】 Because order and pay appear in both processes and are included in the synchronization set, they occur as rendezvous events. Events with different names do not synchronize automatically. If synchronization is required, names must be aligned explicitly or adjusted through renaming.

entries.title: Structuring Communication with Channels
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Structuring Communication with Channels
entries.text: Real systems often contain many interacting processes. CSP uses channels to structure that interaction. A channel is an abstract route over which messages move between processes. 【Context-dependent snippet】 In this example, PRODUCER synchronizes with BUFFER on in, and BUFFER synchronizes with CONSUMER on out. The communication structure is visible in the process composition itself.

entries.title: Building Systems Hierarchically
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Building Systems Hierarchically
entries.text: One of the strengths of CSP is compositionality. Small processes can be combined to form larger systems. This hierarchical construction allows large systems to be decomposed into understandable pieces. 【擬似記法】 This recursive definition constructs a pipeline of arbitrary length.

entries.title: Integrating Choice and Concurrency
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Integrating Choice and Concurrency
entries.text: CSP treats choice and concurrency within one unified framework. External choice (□): the environment determines which branch is taken. 【擬似記法】 Internal choice (⊓): the process chooses nondeterministically. 【擬似記法】 Parallel composition: multiple processes run concurrently. 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: The Benefit of Time Abstraction
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Benefit of Time Abstraction
entries.text: CSP abstracts away from concrete execution time and focuses on the ordering of events. This abstraction makes it possible to analyze essential system behavior without being distracted by low-level timing details. When necessary, timing constraints can still be introduced. Timed variants of CSP make durations and deadlines explicit. But the untimed form is valuable precisely because it isolates the logical structure of concurrency first.

entries.title: Handling Failure and Exceptions
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Handling Failure and Exceptions
entries.text: CSP can express not only normal behavior but also faults and exceptional cases. A fault can be treated as just another kind of event, which means failure handling can be integrated into the same control structure as normal behavior. 【擬似記法】 This unified treatment makes it easier to reason about robustness at the design stage.

entries.title: 6.3 Basic Processes and Their Composition
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: 6.3 Basic Processes and Their Composition
entries.text: 6.3 Basic Processes and Their Composition

entries.title: Primitive Processes
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Primitive Processes
entries.text: All the richer systems described in CSP are built from a small number of basic processes. Understanding these primitives is the foundation of the notation. STOP: a process that does nothing and cannot proceed, representing a deadlock state. 【Context-dependent snippet】 SKIP: a process that terminates successfully. 【Context-dependent snippet】 Simple event processes: 【擬似記法】 These primitives are like notes in music: by combining a small vocabulary, we obtain rich behavior.

entries.title: The Prefix Operator (→)
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Prefix Operator (→)
entries.text: The prefix operator is the most basic constructor in CSP. It means that after a certain event occurs, the process behaves in a specified way. 【擬似記法】 This means that after a coin is inserted, tea or coffee can be selected. A crucial feature of prefix is that the process waits until the specified event can occur. That waiting behavior is where synchronization becomes visible.

entries.title: Choice Operators
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Choice Operators
entries.text: CSP has several forms of choice, and it is important to understand their semantics. External choice (□): the environment chooses. 【擬似記法】 Here, the environment determines whether a customer inserts a card or an administrator enters maintenance mode. Internal choice (⊓): the process chooses nondeterministically. 【擬似記法】 Guarded choice: 【擬似記法】 A guarded branch is available only when its condition holds.

entries.title: Parallel Composition Operators
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Parallel Composition Operators
entries.text: The family of operators for parallel composition is central to CSP. Independent parallel ( · · · ): processes run independently. 【Context-dependent snippet】 These processes do not interact and may proceed independently. Synchronized parallel ([ · X · ]): processes synchronize on a chosen set of events. 【Context-dependent snippet】 The events order and pay must occur jointly, while the others proceed independently. Interleaving: the events of processes may be interwoven in any order. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Pipeline Composition
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Pipeline Composition
entries.text: Pipelines are a standard way to describe staged data flow among processes. 【擬似記法】 The producer, processor, and consumer are connected through intermediate channels.

entries.title: Infinite Processes Through Recursion
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Infinite Processes Through Recursion
entries.text: Many practical systems must run indefinitely. CSP expresses such behavior naturally through recursion. Simple recursion: 【擬似記法】 Recursion with state: 【擬似記法】 Mutual recursion: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Process Families
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Process Families
entries.text: When many similar processes are needed, process families offer a compact way to define them. 【擬似記法】 This defines N worker processes running concurrently.

entries.title: Hiding and Abstraction
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Hiding and Abstraction
