entries.text: To conceal internal events from the external interface, CSP provides the hiding operator (\). 【Context-dependent snippet】 This exposes only the public behavior while suppressing internal details.

entries.title: Renaming
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Renaming
entries.text: CSP also supports renaming, which is useful for reusing generic processes in a more specific setting. 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: Design Principles for Process Composition
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Design Principles for Process Composition
entries.text: Useful principles for composing CSP processes include the following. Minimal interfaces: synchronize only where necessary. Symmetry: use symmetric designs where possible. Reuse: write generic process definitions. Stepwise refinement: move gradually from abstraction to detail. These principles help produce concurrent designs that remain understandable and maintainable.

entries.title: 6.4 Synchronization and Communication
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: 6.4 Synchronization and Communication
entries.text: 6.4 Synchronization and Communication

entries.title: The Essential Meaning of Synchronization
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Essential Meaning of Synchronization
entries.text: Synchronization is one of the core concepts in concurrent systems. In CSP, it means that multiple processes coordinate at a particular event. The analogy is an orchestra following a conductor or dancers moving together to the same beat. CSP uses a rendezvous-style notion of synchronization. An event can occur only when all processes involved in that event are ready. That requirement expresses coordination directly.

entries.title: How Channel Communication Works
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: How Channel Communication Works
entries.text: CSP models communication through channels. Output (!): send a message. 【擬似記法】 Input (?): receive a message. 【擬似記法】 Synchronous communication: send and receive occur together. 【Context-dependent snippet】 In this abstraction, send and receive are one synchronized event. If real systems must model loss, delay, retransmission, or buffering, those phenomena need to be represented explicitly.

entries.title: The Idea of Buffering
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Idea of Buffering
entries.text: Real communication often requires buffering. In CSP, a buffer can itself be modeled as a process. Single-element buffer: 【擬似記法】 Buffer of capacity N: 【擬似記法】 FIFO buffer: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Priority-Based Communication
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Priority-Based Communication
entries.text: Some systems need message priorities. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Broadcast Communication
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Broadcast Communication
entries.text: Sometimes a single message must be delivered to multiple receivers. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Selective Communication
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Selective Communication
entries.text: A router-like process may choose the communication partner based on the message's destination. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Communication with Timeouts
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Communication with Timeouts
entries.text: Real systems also need to cope with delay and failure. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Handshake Protocols
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Handshake Protocols
entries.text: More elaborate communication often requires multi-stage handshakes. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Classes of Synchronization Patterns
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Classes of Synchronization Patterns
entries.text: Synchronization patterns in CSP include the following. One-to-one communication: simple point-to-point messaging One-to-many communication: broadcast or multicast Many-to-one communication: multiple senders to one receiver Many-to-many communication: richer network-style interaction Barrier synchronization: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: The Leader-Follower Pattern
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Leader-Follower Pattern
entries.text: Hierarchical control structures can also be modeled directly. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Voting Protocols
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Voting Protocols
entries.text: Agreement protocols in distributed systems can be captured as CSP processes. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Communication Reliability
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Communication Reliability
entries.text: Practical systems often require explicit handling of communication failures. Message loss: 【擬似記法】 Duplicate elimination: 【擬似記法】 Ordering guarantee: 【擬似記法】 By combining such communication patterns, CSP can describe the behavior of realistic distributed systems in a disciplined way.

entries.title: 6.5 Deadlock and Liveness: The Pathology of Systems
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: 6.5 Deadlock and Liveness: The Pathology of Systems
entries.text: 6.5 Deadlock and Liveness: The Pathology of Systems

entries.title: Deadlock as a Pathological Phenomenon
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Deadlock as a Pathological Phenomenon
entries.text: Deadlock is one of the most serious problems in a concurrent system. Multiple processes wait for one another, and the entire system reaches a state in which no useful progress is possible. A familiar analogy is four cars stuck at an intersection, each waiting for another to move first. In CSP, deadlock can be defined formally. The system is deadlocked when every participating process is waiting for an event that another waiting process would have to initiate. 【擬似記法】 Here, the left process waits for a while the right process waits for b, so neither can move.

entries.title: The Four Conditions for Deadlock
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Four Conditions for Deadlock
entries.text: Classical results say that four conditions are jointly necessary for deadlock. Mutual exclusion: a resource cannot be used simultaneously by multiple processes. Hold and wait: a process holds one resource while waiting for another. No preemption: resources cannot be forcibly taken away. Circular wait: there is a cycle in the wait-for relation. CSP can describe these conditions explicitly. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Deadlock Avoidance Strategies
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Deadlock Avoidance Strategies
entries.text: To avoid deadlock, at least one of the four conditions must be broken. Resource ordering: 【擬似記法】 If all processes acquire resources in the same global order, circular wait can be prevented. Timeout mechanism: 【擬似記法】 Banker's algorithm: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Livelock as a More Subtle Problem
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Livelock as a More Subtle Problem
entries.text: Livelock is different from deadlock. The system is active, but not making real progress. A common analogy is two people in a narrow corridor repeatedly trying to step aside in the same direction. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Guaranteeing Liveness
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Guaranteeing Liveness
entries.text: Liveness means that "something good eventually happens." In CSP, liveness can be analyzed from several perspectives. Starvation freedom: every process eventually makes progress. 【擬似記法】 Fairness: a process that continually has the opportunity to proceed is ultimately allowed to do so. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Formalizing Progress Conditions
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Formalizing Progress Conditions
entries.text: Progress guarantees can also be written abstractly. Minimal progress guarantee: 【擬似記法】 This reads as: always, eventually, a progress event occurs. Bounded waiting: 【擬似記法】 This expresses that after a request, permission is granted within at most n time units.

entries.title: Detection and Recovery
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Detection and Recovery
entries.text: Detection and recovery mechanisms are also important parts of a practical system. Deadlock detector: 【擬似記法】 Recovery strategies: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: The Dining Philosophers Problem
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Dining Philosophers Problem
entries.text: A classic illustration of deadlock is the dining philosophers problem. 【擬似記法】 The example illustrates both a problematic design and two standard remedies: asymmetric acquisition order and waiter-based control.

entries.title: Impact on Performance
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Impact on Performance
entries.text: Deadlock-avoidance measures often have performance costs. Conservative strategies are safe but may reduce efficiency. Optimistic strategies are efficient but may incur higher recovery cost. Adaptive strategies switch behavior depending on load or contention. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Tool-Based Verification
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Tool-Based Verification
entries.text: Dedicated tools can analyze CSP models for deadlock and related properties. FDR (Failures-Divergences Refinement): rigorous model checking and refinement analysis ProB: model checking and animation support PAT: support for richer verification scenarios, including probabilistic reasoning These tools allow deadlock risks to be discovered and corrected while the system is still at the design stage.

entries.title: 6.6 Learning Through an Example: Designing a Restaurant System
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: 6.6 Learning Through an Example: Designing a Restaurant System
entries.text: 6.6 Learning Through an Example: Designing a Restaurant System

entries.title: Organizing the System Requirements
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Organizing the System Requirements
entries.text: A restaurant system is a strong example for concurrent design. Multiple roles — customers, waiters, chefs, and cashiers — must coordinate while sharing limited resources such as tables, kitchen equipment, and ingredients. Main actors: customers; waiters; chefs; cashier; and manager. Shared resources: tables; kitchen equipment; ingredient inventory; and menu information. Main processes: customer arrival and seating; order intake and transmission to the kitchen; cooking and serving; and billing and departure.

entries.title: Defining Basic Processes
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Defining Basic Processes
entries.text: We can begin by describing the basic behavior of each role. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: The Table-Management Subsystem
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Table-Management Subsystem
entries.text: Table assignment is a core subsystem. A limited number of seats must be managed efficiently while avoiding coordination problems. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: The Order-Processing Workflow
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Order-Processing Workflow
entries.text: Order processing from intake through serving can be described as a set of cooperating concurrent processes. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Resource Management for Kitchen Equipment
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Resource Management for Kitchen Equipment
entries.text: Kitchen equipment such as stoves and ovens must be managed with mutual exclusion. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: The Inventory-Management System
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Inventory-Management System
entries.text: Ingredient inventory and replenishment also form an important concurrent subsystem. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: The Payment-Processing System
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: The Payment-Processing System
entries.text: A practical restaurant must also support several payment methods. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Emergency Handling
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Emergency Handling
entries.text: Emergency cases such as food-safety incidents or fire must also be represented. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Integrating the Whole System
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Integrating the Whole System
entries.text: Once the subsystems are defined, they can be composed into one whole system. 【擬似記法】 The following is a conceptual composition example. Because several process definitions are omitted, it should not be treated as executable CSPM as-is.

entries.title: Considering Performance Optimization
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Considering Performance Optimization
entries.text: A practical system must also address performance. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Properties That Should Be Verified
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Properties That Should Be Verified
entries.text: Important verification targets for the restaurant system include the following. Safety: absence of deadlock; correct mutual exclusion on shared resources; and adherence to food-safety rules. Liveness: every customer is eventually served; orders do not remain delayed forever; and equipment is not occupied forever without progress. Fairness: service follows a fair queueing policy; staff workload is balanced; and resources are shared fairly. The restaurant example shows how CSP can be applied to a realistic system that combines concurrency, resource management, communication, and error handling in one framework.

entries.title: End-of-Chapter Exercises
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: End-of-Chapter Exercises
entries.text: If you use AI while working through these exercises Treat AI output as a proposal; use verifiers to make the final judgment. Keep a record of the prompts you used, the generated specifications and invariants, the verification commands and logs (including seed, depth, and scope), and the revision history when counterexamples were found. For detailed templates, see Appendix D and Appendix F.

entries.title: Basic Exercise 1: Reading CSP Notation
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Basic Exercise 1: Reading CSP Notation
entries.text: Read the following CSP process definitions and explain the behavior of the system. 【擬似記法】 Explain: the role and behavior of each process; the synchronization points among the processes; the data flow through the whole system; and an example of a possible execution sequence.

entries.title: Basic Exercise 2: Deadlock Analysis
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Basic Exercise 2: Deadlock Analysis
entries.text: Analyze whether the following system can deadlock. 【擬似記法】 Discuss: a deadlock scenario; the four deadlock conditions and whether they hold; at least two prevention strategies; and a corrected CSP definition.

entries.title: Practical Exercise 1: Designing an ATM System
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Practical Exercise 1: Designing an ATM System
entries.text: Model an ATM system in CSP satisfying the following requirements. Basic functions: card insertion and PIN entry; balance inquiry, withdrawal, and deposit; and receipt printing and card ejection. Concurrency requirements: multiple ATMs access the same banking system; each ATM serves at most one customer at a time; and access to the central banking system must be controlled safely. Safety requirements: prevent unauthorized access; reject withdrawals when funds are insufficient; and stop safely under network failure. Tasks: define the main processes; define the resource-management mechanism; integrate error handling; and compose the whole system.

entries.title: Practical Exercise 2: Using Process Composition
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Practical Exercise 2: Using Process Composition
entries.text: For the ATM system above, extend the design in the following ways. Hierarchical design build composite operations from basic operations; separate the interface layer from the business-logic layer. Concurrency control support concurrent use by multiple customers at different ATMs; add a maintenance mode for administrators. Communication patterns introduce reliable communication between ATMs and the central system; add failure detection and recovery.

entries.title: Advanced Exercise: Designing a Distributed System
entries.chapter: Chapter 6: Process-Centric Specification — Concurrency with CSP
entries.heading: Advanced Exercise: Designing a Distributed System
entries.text: Design a distributed file system in CSP. System structure: multiple client nodes; multiple storage nodes; and redundant metadata servers. Requirements: file read and write operations; data redundancy through replication; tolerance to node failures; and consistency guarantees. Design tasks: inter-node communication protocol; failure detection and recovery; distributed agreement algorithm; and load-balancing mechanism. Properties to verify: absence of deadlock; preservation of data consistency; robustness under partial failure; and fairness, meaning that every client eventually receives service. Through these exercises, you should acquire practical skill in designing concurrent systems with CSP and be prepared for the richer treatment of temporal properties in the next chapter.

entries.title: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.text: Translation status: Partial. Reviewed against Japanese source commit 83d031100ae7 on 2026-07-16. Some content, headings, examples, tables, or references remain partially synchronized. Track the remaining work.

entries.title: 7.1 The Challenge of Distributed Systems: Time and State Become Complex
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: 7.1 The Challenge of Distributed Systems: Time and State Become Complex
entries.text: 7.1 The Challenge of Distributed Systems: Time and State Become Complex

entries.title: The Fundamental Difficulty of Distribution
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Fundamental Difficulty of Distribution
entries.text: Distributed systems are one of the key technical foundations of modern digital society, but their design and implementation involve difficulties that are qualitatively different from those of a single-machine system. Those difficulties arise because physical constraints and logical constraints interact in complicated ways. The most fundamental problem is the ambiguity of time. In a single machine, the execution order of instructions is largely deterministic, so the statement "A executed before B" has a clear meaning. In a distributed system, however, the temporal order between operations running on different machines is not obvious.

entries.title: The Limits of Physical Time
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Limits of Physical Time
entries.text: Each machine has its own local clock, and perfect synchronization among those clocks is impossible. Relativity already tells us that absolute simultaneity is not a physically meaningful concept. Even at a practical engineering level, network delay, clock skew, and timezone differences make it hard to share one precise notion of time. Suppose two servers, one in Tokyo and one in New York, each execute an operation at "12:00:00.000" according to their own clocks. Were those actions truly simultaneous? Once we account for timezone offsets, network delay, and clock precision, the answer becomes uncertain.

entries.title: Increasing Complexity of Causality
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Increasing Complexity of Causality
entries.text: In a distributed system, causal relationships among events become more subtle. For event A to be the cause of event B, information about A must have reached B. Because information propagation takes time, physical timestamps alone are not enough. 【擬似記法】 To reason about such situations, we need a notion of logical time, not just physical time.

entries.title: The Reality of Partial Failure
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Reality of Partial Failure
entries.text: Partial failure is unavoidable in distributed systems. One part of the system may fail while the rest continues to operate. This is very different from the usual failure mode of a single-machine program. Typical examples of partial failure include the following. Network partition: communication between some nodes is lost. Node failure: a particular machine stops responding. Performance degradation: one part of the system becomes much slower. Byzantine failure: a node behaves arbitrarily or maliciously. Maintaining consistency under such conditions is one of the core design problems of distributed systems.

entries.title: The Trade-Off Between Consistency and Availability
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Trade-Off Between Consistency and Availability
entries.text: One of the most important results in distributed-systems theory is the CAP theorem as formalized by Gilbert and Lynch. The theorem assumes an asynchronous model in which a network partition may cause message loss. Under that assumption, an execution experiencing a partition cannot guarantee both of the following. Consistency: atomic, or linearizable, behavior in which each operation appears to take effect at one point between its invocation and response. Availability: every request received by a non-failing node eventually receives a response. Here, partition tolerance is not a third guarantee to maximize independently; it is the assumption that the model permits such partitions. CAP is therefore not a rule that a system must choose one of three letters at all times. During a partition, a request may be delayed or rejected to preserve linearizability, or it may be answered while temporarily providing a weaker consistency model. The relevant operations, failure and communication model, and required consistency level must be stated explicitly.

entries.title: Managing Nondeterminism
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Managing Nondeterminism
entries.text: Nondeterminism is an essential feature of distributed systems. Even from the same initial state, different network delays or scheduling decisions may lead to different outcomes. That nondeterminism gives flexibility, but it also makes correctness harder to guarantee. We need to ensure that the system remains safe under all relevant execution orders, not just a convenient one.

entries.title: The Difficulty of Consensus
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Difficulty of Consensus
entries.text: Distributed systems repeatedly face the need for agreement. Multiple nodes must agree on a value, a leader, or an ordering of operations. That turns out to be harder than it first appears. The FLP impossibility result assumes a completely asynchronous model with no known upper bound on process speed or message delay, deterministic processes, and at most one crash-stop failure. Under those assumptions, no consensus protocol guarantees termination in every admissible execution, even though a message sent to a non-failing process is eventually delivered. FLP does not say that safety and termination are both unachievable in every execution; it limits a deterministic, universal termination guarantee under its stated model. Practical protocols change those assumptions. Partial synchrony adds timing bounds that hold eventually, failure detectors expose structured suspicions, and randomized algorithms replace deterministic termination with a probabilistic guarantee. An impossibility theorem should therefore be read as a package of computational, failure, communication, and guarantee assumptions, not as a conclusion in isolation.

entries.title: State Explosion
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: State Explosion
entries.text: The state space of a distributed system grows exponentially with the number of nodes. If each of N nodes has K possible local states, then the combined system may have K^N global states. This state explosion makes it hard to understand all possible behaviors by informal reasoning alone. Formal methods are therefore essential for controlling complexity and guaranteeing critical properties.

entries.title: TLA+ as an Approach to These Problems
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: TLA+ as an Approach to These Problems
entries.text: TLA+ (Temporal Logic of Actions) addresses these difficulties through a few key ideas. Temporal logic for dynamic properties: properties such as "always" and "eventually" can be written precisely. State-transition modeling: a system is abstracted as states and the transitions among them. Actions as structured change: state change is described explicitly as an action. Fairness assumptions for realistic modeling: the specification can rule out unrealistic executions caused only by pathological scheduling. TLA+ does not make the essential difficulties of distributed systems disappear. Instead, it makes them explicit and therefore analyzable.

entries.title: 7.2 The Philosophy of TLA+: Actions and Temporal Logic
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: 7.2 The Philosophy of TLA+: Actions and Temporal Logic
entries.text: 7.2 The Philosophy of TLA+: Actions and Temporal Logic

entries.title: Leslie Lamport's Central Insight
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Leslie Lamport's Central Insight
entries.text: One of Leslie Lamport's most important insights was that system behavior can be understood as a sequence of states. That idea seems simple, but it has major consequences. Traditional programming often focuses on the execution of commands. Lamport's view shifts attention from "which instruction ran" to "how the system state changed." Once that viewpoint is adopted, even complicated distributed behavior can be understood in a uniform way.

entries.title: A Worldview Based on State-Transition Systems
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: A Worldview Based on State-Transition Systems
entries.text: In TLA+, every system is treated as a state-transition system. The system starts from an initial state and evolves through a sequence of transitions. 【擬似記法】 The state contains every relevant piece of information about the system: the values of program variables, the status of the network, the local state of each node, and any other information that can influence behavior.

entries.title: Actions as Abstractions of Change
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Actions as Abstractions of Change
entries.text: In TLA+, an action describes a relationship between a current state and a next state. Formally, an action is a predicate over unprimed and primed variables. For example, an action that increments x can be written as follows. 【擬似記法】 Here x denotes the value in the current state and x' denotes the value in the next state.

entries.title: The Expressive Power of Temporal Logic
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Expressive Power of Temporal Logic
entries.text: TLA+ uses temporal logic to express dynamic properties of behavior. TLA+ uses state predicates and actions to describe transitions, then temporal formulas to describe properties of complete behaviors. The basic notation for behavior properties, together with the fairness assumptions used for liveness, is: []P (always): P holds in every state; <>P (eventually): P holds in some future state; P > Q (leads-to): whenever P holds, Q eventually follows, an abbreviation for [](P => <>Q); and WFvars(A) / SFvars(A): weak and strong fairness assumptions for action A. This book also uses □ / ◇ as mathematical typography, while TLA+ input uses [] / <>. 【Pseudo notation】 The first formula says that the process becomes ready infinitely often. The second says that the system eventually reaches a stable condition and stays there. The third says that each state satisfying request is eventually followed by one satisfying response.

entries.title: Distinguishing LTL Next from TLA+ Prime
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Distinguishing LTL Next from TLA+ Prime
entries.text: The LTL formula X P, often typeset as ○P, evaluates P at the next position of a trace. TLA+ prime notation is different: x' refers to the next-state value of x inside an action, which is a predicate over a pair of states. Ordinary TLA+ specifications are structured around a stuttering-permitting formula such as Init /\ [][Next]vars /\ Liveness. This chapter therefore uses actions with primes plus [], <>, >, and fairness as its entry points, rather than presenting a next-time operator as a primary TLA+ operator.

entries.title: The Power of Prime Notation
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Power of Prime Notation
entries.text: Prime notation is one of the cleanest ideas in TLA+. It distinguishes the current state from the next state concisely and directly. 【擬似記法】 This reads naturally as a state change: the amount is positive, the source account has enough funds, and then the two balances are updated accordingly.

entries.title: The Meaning of Fairness
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Meaning of Fairness
entries.text: In a concurrent or distributed system, an action may be enabled forever in the specification and yet never be scheduled in some pathological execution. Fairness is a way to rule out such unrealistic behaviors by assumption. It does not mean that an implementation automatically becomes fair merely because the specification includes fairness. Here ENABLED A means that from the current state, some next state exists that satisfies action A. Weak fairness (WF): if an action is continuously enabled from some point onward, then it cannot be ignored forever. If it remains enabled, it must occur repeatedly. Strong fairness (SF): if an action becomes enabled infinitely often, then it cannot be avoided forever. Because this includes intermittent enablement, strong fairness is generally a stronger assumption than weak fairness, although the two can coincide for a particular action and specification. The following fragment still needs declarations for vars, the actions, Init, and Next, with the fair actions defined as subactions of Next; it is not a complete module that runs on its own. 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: Compositionality
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Compositionality
entries.text: TLA+ supports compositionality. Larger systems can be built from smaller specifications. 【擬似記法】 Each process can be specified independently, then combined using logical conjunction.

entries.title: Stepwise Refinement
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Stepwise Refinement
entries.text: TLA+ also supports refinement. Development can proceed from an abstract design to a more concrete specification, while the proof obligation runs from the concrete behavior to the abstract behavior it must implement. 【Pseudo notation】 The notation above is explanatory rather than an executable TLA+ module. A real refinement proof applies a refinement mapping from abstract variables to expressions over the concrete state and hides internal variables before proving the implication. The mapping and hiding boundary are therefore part of the claim, not incidental implementation details.

entries.title: Accepting Nondeterminism
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Accepting Nondeterminism
entries.text: TLA+ does not treat nondeterminism as a flaw to be eliminated. Instead, it is a valuable modeling tool. By allowing different actions to be chosen nondeterministically, the specification can preserve implementation freedom while still enforcing the required safety properties. 【擬似記法】 The specification does not force one branch to be chosen. Instead, it requires all possible choices to be safe.

entries.title: Putting Mathematical Foundations to Work
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Putting Mathematical Foundations to Work
entries.text: TLA+ is built on set theory, logic, and computation theory, but it integrates those foundations into a notation designed for system engineering. Because it can work naturally with sets, functions, sequences, and quantified formulas, it scales from abstract models to detailed protocol behavior.

entries.title: Integration with Tools
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Integration with Tools
entries.text: TLA+ is not just a description language. It is tightly integrated with tooling, most notably TLC, the TLA+ model checker. That integration is one of its most important practical strengths: a mathematically written specification can be checked automatically against concrete finite models.

entries.title: 7.3 State and Action: The Dynamic Model of a System
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: 7.3 State and Action: The Dynamic Model of a System
entries.text: 7.3 State and Action: The Dynamic Model of a System

entries.title: Structuring the State Space
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Structuring the State Space
entries.text: In TLA+, a state represents the complete relevant information about the system. This may include program variables, network state, local node state, and any other information that affects behavior. A state can often be understood as a function from variables to values. For example, a banking system might be modeled as follows. 【擬似記法】 This gives a compact description of the full situation at one moment.

entries.title: Defining Variables and Types
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Defining Variables and Types
entries.text: TLA+ makes the state variables explicit, and type-like invariants can restrict them to the values that make sense for the model. 【擬似記法】 Although TLA+ itself is untyped, such invariants play the role of a type discipline for the model.

entries.title: Specifying the Initial State
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Specifying the Initial State
entries.text: Behavior begins from Init, the predicate describing the initial state. 【擬似記法】 The initial state fixes what is true at the start of every behavior.

entries.title: State Change Through Actions
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: State Change Through Actions
entries.text: An action describes one way the state can evolve. Deposit example: 【擬似記法】 This says that a deposit increases the selected balance, appends a log entry, and advances the clock, while leaving pending unchanged.

entries.title: The Complexity of a Transfer Operation
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Complexity of a Transfer Operation
entries.text: Transfers are more complex because several parts of the state must be updated consistently. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Conditional Actions
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Conditional Actions
entries.text: Many actions are meaningful only under certain conditions. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Nondeterministic Choice
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Nondeterministic Choice
entries.text: A TLA+ next-state relation often combines many possible actions. 【擬似記法】 The system may take any of these actions, provided the chosen action's constraints are satisfied.

entries.title: The Importance of Frame Conditions
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Importance of Frame Conditions
entries.text: When an action is written, it is important to make clear which variables do not change. These frame conditions prevent unintended side effects. 【擬似記法】 A read-only action should explicitly state that all relevant variables remain unchanged.

entries.title: Expressing Atomicity
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Expressing Atomicity
entries.text: When several changes must happen together, they are written in one action. This is how atomicity is expressed. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Modeling the Passage of Time
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Modeling the Passage of Time
entries.text: Some systems require explicit representation of time passing. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Interacting with the Environment
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Interacting with the Environment
entries.text: A system usually interacts with clients, users, or other external components. That interaction can also be modeled as actions. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Modeling Failures
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Modeling Failures
entries.text: Practical models often need explicit failure actions. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Constraints as State Predicates
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Constraints as State Predicates
entries.text: Desired properties can also be written as state predicates and invariants. 【擬似記法】 This kind of formula captures the consistency properties that every reachable state or transition should respect.

entries.title: 7.4 Temporal Logic: A Language for "When" and "How"
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: 7.4 Temporal Logic: A Language for "When" and "How"
entries.text: 7.4 Temporal Logic: A Language for "When" and "How"

entries.title: Abstracting Time
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Abstracting Time
entries.text: Temporal logic is a logical system for reasoning about time. In TLA+, time is abstracted as a sequence of states rather than treated primarily as physical clock time. This makes it possible to describe essential temporal properties without relying on one concrete timestamping scheme. The power of temporal logic lies in its focus on order and inevitability rather than on precise wall-clock values.

entries.title: Basic Temporal Operators
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Basic Temporal Operators
entries.text: TLA+ uses several foundational temporal operators. □ (always): 【擬似記法】 Example: 【擬似記法】 ◇ (eventually): 【擬似記法】 Example: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Combining Operators
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Combining Operators
entries.text: Temporal operators can be combined to express richer long-run behavior. □◇P: P holds infinitely often. 【擬似記法】 ◇□P: eventually P becomes permanently true. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: The Leads-To Relation
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Leads-To Relation
entries.text: A very common temporal idea is that whenever A happens, B eventually follows. 【擬似記法】 Example: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Distinguishing Safety from Liveness
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Distinguishing Safety from Liveness
entries.text: Temporal properties are often divided into two main categories. Safety: nothing bad ever happens. 【擬似記法】 Examples: 【擬似記法】 Liveness: something good eventually happens. 【擬似記法】 Examples: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Temporal Expressions of Fairness
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Temporal Expressions of Fairness
entries.text: Fairness can also be written precisely in temporal form. Weak fairness: 【擬似記法】 This says that if action A eventually remains enabled forever, it cannot be ignored forever. If it remains enabled, it must occur infinitely often. Strong fairness: 【擬似記法】 This says that if A becomes enabled infinitely often, then it must also occur infinitely often. Since intermittent enablement is enough, this is generally a stronger assumption than weak fairness, although the two can coincide for a particular action and specification. Note that ⟨A⟩vars excludes stuttering by requiring that action A occurs and that the tuple vars actually changes.

entries.title: Temporal Properties of Mutual Exclusion
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Temporal Properties of Mutual Exclusion
entries.text: Mutual exclusion is a standard example of temporal reasoning. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Temporal Properties of Distributed Consensus
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Temporal Properties of Distributed Consensus
entries.text: Consensus algorithms require several classic temporal properties. Agreement: 【擬似記法】 Validity: 【擬似記法】 Termination: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Temporal Expressions of Causality
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Temporal Expressions of Causality
entries.text: Distributed systems also require temporal reasoning about causal dependence. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Expressing Time Bounds
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Expressing Time Bounds
entries.text: Practical systems often require bounded-response guarantees. 【擬似記法】 Some of these formulas are explanatory and use notation that is not standard raw TLA+ syntax. They should therefore be read as pseudo notation.

entries.title: Temporal Models of Failure and Recovery
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Temporal Models of Failure and Recovery
entries.text: Failures and recoveries are also naturally expressed as temporal requirements. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: More Complex Temporal Properties
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: More Complex Temporal Properties
entries.text: Real systems usually require several temporal concerns to be combined. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: The Power and Limits of Temporal Logic
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Power and Limits of Temporal Logic
entries.text: Temporal logic is powerful, but not omnipotent. It expresses well: safety and liveness; fairness and progress; causal and ordering constraints; and recurring or eventual patterns of behavior. Its limits include: quantitative real-time reasoning in full generality; probabilistic guarantees; explicit resource-consumption analysis; and highly detailed numeric optimization. The key is to choose an abstraction level at which the temporal properties are both meaningful and analyzable.

entries.title: 7.5 Practical Model Checking with TLC
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: 7.5 Practical Model Checking with TLC
entries.text: 7.5 Practical Model Checking with TLC

entries.title: What TLC Is
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: What TLC Is
entries.text: TLC (the TLA+ model checker) is the principal analysis tool for executable TLA+ specifications. Developed around Leslie Lamport's work, it explores behaviors of a finite model automatically and checks whether desired properties hold. The main value of TLC is that it makes mathematical specifications operational. Problems that are hard to spot from formulas alone often appear immediately as concrete counterexamples.

entries.title: The Basic Principle of Model Checking
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Basic Principle of Model Checking
entries.text: Model checking systematically explores the state space of the system and asks whether any reachable state or behavior violates the properties of interest. TLC follows a pattern such as this: generate all states satisfying Init; compute successors using Next; check invariants and temporal properties on the reachable states and behaviors; and report a counterexample trace if a violation is found.

entries.title: Executability of a Specification
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Executability of a Specification
entries.text: For TLC to analyze a specification, the model must be executable in a finite sense. That means restricting an abstract specification to finite sets and finite bounds that TLC can enumerate. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Creating a Configuration File
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Creating a Configuration File
entries.text: TLC is controlled through a configuration file (.cfg) that supplies concrete constant values, the specification to check, and the properties to verify. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: A Stepwise Verification Strategy
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: A Stepwise Verification Strategy
entries.text: A staged approach is effective for nontrivial systems. Stage 1: type-style invariants 【擬似記法】 Stage 2: safety invariants 【擬似記法】 Stage 3: liveness properties 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Controlling the State Space
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Controlling the State Space
entries.text: TLC's usefulness depends heavily on the size of the explored state space. Good control techniques therefore matter. Using symmetry: 【Context-dependent snippet】 This is typically used in a model or configuration context to reduce the number of symmetric states that TLC explores. Adding state constraints: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Analyzing Error Traces
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Analyzing Error Traces
entries.text: When TLC finds a violation, it reports the execution trace that leads to it. Analyzing that trace is often the fastest route to understanding the root cause. Typical output example: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Detecting Deadlock
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Detecting Deadlock
entries.text: TLC can automatically report deadlock when the system reaches a state in which no enabled transition exists. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Verifying Liveness Properties
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Verifying Liveness Properties
entries.text: Checking liveness is generally more expensive than checking safety. TLC uses specialized graph analysis techniques, such as strongly connected components, to search for liveness violations. 【擬似記法】 A TLC violation report for such a property will typically show a cycle in which a desired event is postponed forever.

entries.title: Probabilistic or Sampling-Based Checking
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Probabilistic or Sampling-Based Checking
entries.text: For very large state spaces, exhaustive checking may be impractical. In such cases, bounded or sampling-oriented approaches may be used operationally, even though their guarantees differ from exhaustive model checking. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Performance-Optimization Techniques
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Performance-Optimization Techniques
entries.text: Several practical techniques improve TLC performance. Parallel execution: 【Context-dependent snippet】 Memory tuning: 【Context-dependent snippet】 Checkpointing: 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: Iterative Specification Improvement
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Iterative Specification Improvement
entries.text: In practice, TLC is usually used in an iterative loop. write an initial specification; run TLC; analyze the error or counterexample; improve the specification; and run TLC again. This cycle gradually improves both the model and the team's understanding of the system.

entries.title: Practical Verification Strategy
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Practical Verification Strategy
entries.text: Useful practical tactics include the following. Start small: begin with the smallest model that can express the core problem. Expand gradually: add more detail step by step. Check continuously: rerun verification when the specification changes. Monitor performance: record runtime and state counts to detect scaling issues early.

entries.title: Understanding the Limits of TLC
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Understanding the Limits of TLC
entries.text: TLC also has clear limitations. State explosion: the state space can grow exponentially. Need for finite models: infinite sets cannot be handled directly. Time cost: large models may take a long time to check. Memory cost: the number of states is limited by available memory. Practical use of TLC depends on extracting the maximum value within those constraints.

entries.title: Executable Example Contracts
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Executable Example Contracts
entries.text: The TLA+ fragments in this chapter, especially formulas such as WFvars(Action) and abstract Next sketches, remain context-dependent snippets unless they are paired with the surrounding VARIABLES, Spec, and configuration file. CI-backed executability is limited to the contract blocks below, which invoke the shared JA/EN canonical assets under examples/ through the manifest runner. These chapter-level contracts define the pr-quick baseline, while nightly reruns the same canonical assets with deeper exploration settings. Normalized logs, commands, exit codes, stdout, and stderr are expected under .artifacts/manifest//, and readers should retrieve the examples/ files from the same repository revision instead of copying inline snippets alone. tlc-queue-bounded: canonical assets examples/tla/QueueBounded.tla and examples/tla/QueueBounded.cfg; lane pr-quick; pinned version TLC 1.7.4; expected success marker Model checking completed. No error has been found.. Retrieve both the specification and its .cfg from the same repository revision. 【Tool-compliant (runs as-is)】 apalache-counter: canonical assets examples/apalache/Counter.tla and examples/apalache/Counter.cfg; lane pr-quick; pinned version Apalache 0.52.1; expected success marker Checker reports no error up to computation length 10. Retrieve the whole examples/apalache/ pair from the same commit instead of copying the inline sketch alone. 【Tool-compliant (runs as-is)】

entries.title: 7.6 Applying TLA+ to the Real World: A Distributed Consensus Algorithm
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: 7.6 Applying TLA+ to the Real World: A Distributed Consensus Algorithm
entries.text: 7.6 Applying TLA+ to the Real World: A Distributed Consensus Algorithm

entries.title: The Essence of the Distributed Consensus Problem
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Essence of the Distributed Consensus Problem
entries.text: Distributed consensus is one of the most fundamental problems in distributed systems. Multiple nodes must agree on a value or decision despite failures, message delay, and uncertainty about the state of the rest of the system. The challenge is not only to reach agreement, but to do so in a way that keeps the decision stable once made.

entries.title: A Formal Definition of Consensus
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: A Formal Definition of Consensus
entries.text: A distributed consensus algorithm is expected to satisfy three standard properties. Agreement: any nodes that decide must decide the same value. Validity: the chosen value must have been proposed by some node. Termination: all non-faulty nodes eventually decide. In TLA+, we can express them as follows. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Overview of Raft
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Overview of Raft
entries.text: Raft is a distributed consensus algorithm designed with understandability in mind. It separates the problem into leader election and log replication, which makes the design easier to reason about. Its central concepts include: roles: Leader, Follower, and Candidate; terms: monotonically increasing epochs of leadership; and log entries: the ordered operations that the cluster agrees on.

entries.title: The State Model of Raft
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The State Model of Raft
entries.text: A TLA+ model of Raft can make all of these pieces explicit. 【Context-dependent snippet】

entries.title: Defining the Initial State
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Defining the Initial State
entries.text: Initially, all servers begin as followers. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: The Leader-Election Process
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: The Leader-Election Process
entries.text: Raft leader election can be written in stages. Election timeout: 【擬似記法】 Sending vote requests: 【擬似記法】 Processing vote responses: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Log Replication
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Log Replication
entries.text: Once a leader exists, it accepts client requests and replicates them to the followers as log entries. Appending an entry: 【擬似記法】 Sending AppendEntries: 【擬似記法】 Processing responses and committing: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Safety Guarantees
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Safety Guarantees
entries.text: Raft should satisfy several important safety invariants. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Incorporating a Failure Model
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Incorporating a Failure Model
entries.text: A realistic distributed-system model must also include failures. Node failure: 【擬似記法】 Network partition: 【擬似記法】 Message loss: 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Liveness Guarantees
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Liveness Guarantees
entries.text: Raft also aims to satisfy progress properties, subject to fairness and network assumptions. 【擬似記法】

entries.title: Verifying with TLC
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Verifying with TLC
entries.text: A TLC configuration for a small Raft model might look as follows. 【擬似記法】 This kind of model lets us search for counterexamples within the configured small cluster, fault scenarios, properties, and fairness assumptions. Even a completed run does not establish correctness of the implementation, omitted failures, or larger configurations. The broader lesson is that even a complicated protocol such as distributed consensus can have its assumptions and properties written precisely in TLA+ and checked over a finite model with TLC. The resulting evidence should be connected to implementation review, testing, and operational fault handling.

entries.title: End-of-Chapter Exercises
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: End-of-Chapter Exercises
entries.text: If you use AI while working through these exercises Treat AI output as a proposal; use verifiers to make the final judgment. Keep a record of the prompts you used, the generated specifications and invariants, the verification commands and logs (including seed, depth, and scope), and the revision history when counterexamples were found. For detailed templates, see Appendix D and Appendix F.

entries.title: Basic Exercise 1: Understanding Temporal-Logic Notation
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Basic Exercise 1: Understanding Temporal-Logic Notation
entries.text: Explain the following temporal formulas in plain language and give a concrete example of each in a real system. □(resourcerequested ⟹ ◇resourcegranted) ◇□(systemstable) □◇(garbagecollection) (criticalsectionentered) > (criticalsectionexited) For each formula, provide: an explanation of its meaning; a concrete real-system example; and an example of a situation in which the property is violated.

entries.title: Basic Exercise 2: Describing State and Action
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Basic Exercise 2: Describing State and Action
entries.text: Describe the following system in terms of TLA+ state and actions. Simple inventory-management system: a variable stock stores the current quantity; a restock operation increases the quantity; a shipment operation decreases the quantity but cannot execute when stock is insufficient; and a stocktaking operation corrects the quantity to the accurate value. Describe: the variable declarations and type invariant; the initial state; the action definition for each operation; the Next predicate; and a basic safety invariant.

entries.title: Practical Exercise 1: Designing a Mutual-Exclusion Protocol
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Practical Exercise 1: Designing a Mutual-Exclusion Protocol
entries.text: Write Peterson's algorithm in TLA+ and verify it with TLC. Requirements: two processes use mutual exclusion; each process has the states "noncritical," "trying," and "critical"; and the protocol is controlled by a flag array and a turn variable. Properties to verify: mutual exclusion: the two processes are never in the critical section at the same time; progress: if there is a pending request, eventually some process enters the critical section; and fairness: both processes can enter the critical section repeatedly rather than one starving forever.

entries.title: Practical Exercise 2: The Producer-Consumer Problem
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Practical Exercise 2: The Producer-Consumer Problem
entries.text: Design a producer-consumer system with a finite buffer in TLA+. System structure: multiple producer processes; multiple consumer processes; and a shared fixed-size buffer. Constraints: producers must wait when the buffer is full; consumers must wait when the buffer is empty; and FIFO order must be preserved. Verification targets: absence of deadlock; no data loss; fairness between producers and consumers; and avoidance of buffer overflow and underflow.

entries.title: Advanced Exercise: Designing a Distributed System
entries.chapter: Chapter 7: Specifying Time — Introduction to TLA+
entries.heading: Advanced Exercise: Designing a Distributed System
entries.text: Design a simplified distributed file system in TLA+. System requirements: multiple storage nodes; replication for redundancy; client read and write requests; and tolerance to node failure. Main operations: file creation and deletion; file reading and writing; replication management; and failure detection and recovery. Properties to verify: Consistency: all replicas eventually hold the same contents. Availability: service continues despite some node failures. Durability: data remains persistently stored. Partition tolerance: the system behaves appropriately under network partition. Design questions: How is the failure model defined? Which consistency model is chosen: strong consistency or eventual consistency? Is distributed consensus required, and if so, which algorithm should be chosen? How are performance trade-offs handled? Through these exercises, you should gain practical skill in formal distributed system design with TLA+. In the advanced exercise in particular, it is important to reason explicitly about the implications of CAP-style constraints and the trade-offs between theory and practice.

entries.title: Chapter 8: Introduction to Model Checking
entries.heading: Chapter 8: Introduction to Model Checking
entries.text: Translation status: Partial. Reviewed against Japanese source commit 83d031100ae7 on 2026-07-16. Some content, headings, examples, tables, or references remain partially synchronized. Track the remaining work.

entries.title: 8.1 The Dream of Automated Verification: Letting Computers Prove Correctness
entries.heading: 8.1 The Dream of Automated Verification: Letting Computers Prove Correctness
entries.text: 8.1 The Dream of Automated Verification: Letting Computers Prove Correctness

entries.title: Human Limits and Machine Capability
entries.heading: Human Limits and Machine Capability
entries.text: As software systems become more complex, it becomes increasingly difficult to guarantee correctness through human intuition and experience alone. Developers write code, reviewers inspect it, and testers execute it, but subtle bugs and unexpected interactions still slip through. Model checking complements these cognitive limits with computation. For a specified finite-state model, it systematically explores executions reachable from the configured initial states and checks the selected properties. Its exhaustiveness is relative to that model, property, search method, and configuration, not to the implementation and physical environment as a whole. The key innovation of model checking is automated checking and counterexample search. Once the model and properties are described, a checker either confirms the property for its completed scope or produces a violating trace. A timeout, unknown, resource exhaustion, bounded run, or approximate run is not the same as confirmation.

entries.title: The Power of Exhaustive Exploration
entries.heading: The Power of Exhaustive Exploration
entries.text: The basic idea behind model checking is brute-force exploration of the system's state space. At first glance this may appear inefficient, but in practice it is extremely powerful. Like search-based game programs, model checkers traverse mechanically defined states and transitions. Neither class of program enumerates every real-world possibility without limits; the modeled state space and configuration determine what is searched. In verification, a reproducible result is therefore either a counterexample or evidence that no violation was found in the completed scope. The latter does not cover inputs, implementation details, or failures omitted from the model.

entries.title: Automating Formal Verification
entries.heading: Automating Formal Verification
entries.text: Before model checking became practical, formal verification relied primarily on manual theorem proving. Experts advanced one proof obligation at a time through mathematical reasoning. The method was theoretically strong, but it had clear practical limits: Demand for expertise: advanced mathematical background was required Time and cost: large proofs often took a long time Human fallibility: hand-written proofs can themselves contain mistakes Scalability limits: large systems were difficult to handle Model checking changed the situation fundamentally. A team no longer needed to master every proof detail. If the system model and target properties could be described precisely, verification became possible.

entries.title: The Educational Value of Counterexamples
entries.heading: The Educational Value of Counterexamples
entries.text: One of the most valuable features of model checking is the counterexample it provides when a property does not hold. Instead of returning only an abstract "incorrect," it explains under what circumstances the problem occurs. For system designers, counterexamples are highly actionable: Concrete problem framing: an abstract issue becomes a concrete scenario Cause identification: the root cause can be traced Repair guidance: the failure point becomes visible Deeper understanding: system behavior becomes easier to reason about

entries.title: Improving Quality Through Early Detection
entries.heading: Improving Quality Through Early Detection
entries.text: Model checking can discover problems early in development. By checking designs or specifications before implementation, teams can reduce the number of defects found only after code is written. The cost of fixing a bug generally rises the later it is discovered. Common industry estimates say that if a defect found in requirements costs 1 unit to fix, the same defect may cost 5 units in design, 10 in implementation, 20 in testing, and over 100 in production. The actual multiplier depends on the organization, domain, and process, but the direction is consistent. Early defect detection through model checking is therefore not only a technical advantage but also a cost-control mechanism. Figure 8-1: The overall process of model checking

entries.title: Understanding the Limits Correctly
entries.heading: Understanding the Limits Correctly
entries.text: Model checking is powerful, but it is not universal. Using it effectively requires understanding its limits: The state-explosion problem: the number of states can grow exponentially, making verification difficult The need for abstraction: real systems must be abstracted into finite models The difficulty of property specification: teams need skill in writing formal properties Effects of abstraction and search mode: over-approximation can produce behaviors absent from the implementation, while under-approximation, bounded search, and approximate search can miss violations outside their scope Distinct completion results: completion, counterexample, timeout, unknown, and resource exhaustion must not be reported as one kind of success Practical model checking depends on choosing an appropriate abstraction level and a verification strategy that matches the problem. Reports should record the model and property, tool version, fairness, bounds, approximation mode, timeout, and completion status.

entries.title: Industrial Success Stories
entries.heading: Industrial Success Stories
entries.text: Model checking is no longer only an academic technique. It has a substantial record in industry: Intel uses formal verification in processor design and has found bugs beyond the reach of conventional simulation Microsoft has applied verification to Windows drivers and reduced serious failures such as blue-screen crashes Amazon is well known for using TLA+ and model-checking-oriented thinking when designing distributed systems Airbus uses formal techniques to support assurance arguments for safety-critical control systems These cases show that model checking has matured from a research method into a practical engineering tool.

entries.title: Connecting to the Alloy Models in Chapter 4
entries.heading: Connecting to the Alloy Models in Chapter 4
entries.text: The Alloy modeling you studied in Chapter 4 is an excellent introduction to model checking. Alloy's check command is, in essence, a small-scale form of model checking: Pseudo notation (the Chapter 4 model definitions are omitted, so this fragment cannot be executed as-is): In this check operation, Alloy explores all possible states within the given scope and searches for a counterexample. That is the same core idea you study in this chapter.

entries.title: Democratizing Formal Verification
entries.heading: Democratizing Formal Verification
entries.text: One of model checking's most important contributions is the democratization of formal verification. What used to be accessible only to a small number of specialists has become a comparatively learnable tool for a broader range of engineers. That shift changes formal methods from a niche academic technique into a practical development tool. Over time, model checking is likely to become a standard part of software quality assurance.

entries.title: 8.2 State Spaces and How to Explore Them
entries.heading: 8.2 State Spaces and How to Explore Them
entries.text: 8.2 State Spaces and How to Explore Them

entries.title: State Space as a Conceptual Framework
entries.heading: State Space as a Conceptual Framework
entries.text: A state space is the set of all states a system can possibly take. This mathematical abstraction lets us understand even complex software as a finite or countably infinite collection of points. Consider a running program. The program counter, variable values, memory contents, input/output status, and other runtime information together form the current state. Execution can then be understood as a trajectory through that state space. This idea is analogous to phase spaces in physics and other mathematical representations of dynamical systems. By recasting dynamic behavior as a static structure, we gain something we can analyze.

entries.title: Structure of a State-Transition Graph
entries.heading: Structure of a State-Transition Graph
entries.text: The structure of a state space is usually visualized as a state-transition graph. In such a graph, nodes represent states and edges represent transitions. Pseudo notation: This structure lets us reason about important system properties: Reachability: can one state reach another? Cyclicity: does the system contain persistent loops? Deadlock: is there a state from which no progress is possible? Liveness: is some kind of forward movement always possible?

entries.title: Exploding State Counts
entries.heading: Exploding State Counts
entries.text: In real software systems, the number of states grows explosively. This is the best-known challenge in model checking: the state-explosion problem. Consider a simple example with three Boolean variables: a, b, c ∈ {true, false} Number of possible states = 2^3 = 8 If the system has ten Boolean variables: Number of possible states = 2^10 = 1,024 With 32 Boolean variables: Number of possible states = 2^32 ≈ 4.3 × 10^9 Real programs also contain integers, arrays, pointers, buffers, counters, and various control states. A single 64-bit integer variable already allows 2^64 ≈ 1.8 × 10^19 values.

entries.title: Reachability Analysis
entries.heading: Reachability Analysis
entries.text: Not every state is reachable during actual execution. Model checking therefore focuses on reachable states, that is, states reachable from the initial state. This reduces the search space to some extent. Pseudo notation: This is breadth-first exploration of the reachable state space.

entries.title: Depth-First Versus Breadth-First Search
entries.heading: Depth-First Versus Breadth-First Search
entries.text: State-space exploration typically uses one of two basic strategies. Depth-first search (DFS): Advantage: low memory usage Disadvantage: may fail to find the shortest counterexample Typical use: cycle detection and deadlock detection Breadth-first search (BFS): Advantage: finds the shortest counterexample Disadvantage: high memory usage Typical use: minimum-step reachability analysis Practical tools often switch strategies or combine them depending on the verification objective.

entries.title: Efficient State Representation
entries.heading: Efficient State Representation
entries.text: Representing and managing huge numbers of states efficiently is essential for practical model checking. Bit-vector representation: Pseudo notation: Hash tables: Visited states are typically stored in hash tables to support fast lookup. Compression: Similar states or infrequent patterns can sometimes be compressed to save memory.

entries.title: Partial Order Reduction
entries.heading: Partial Order Reduction
entries.text: In concurrent systems, different execution orders can lead to the same result. Partial order reduction exploits this redundancy. Suppose two operations A and B are independent: execution order A -> B execution order B -> A If both produce the same outcome, it is unnecessary to explore both. By keeping only a representative ordering, the tool reduces the state count.

entries.title: Symbolic Model Checking
entries.heading: Symbolic Model Checking
entries.text: Traditional explicit-state model checking stores states one by one. Symbolic model checking represents sets of states with logical formulas, often using BDD (Binary Decision Diagram) structures. Pseudo notation: When the structure is favorable, symbolic methods can represent extremely large state sets compactly.

entries.title: Iterative Deepening
entries.heading: Iterative Deepening
entries.text: To balance completeness and memory constraints, tools sometimes use iterative deepening. Pseudo notation: This expands the search horizon gradually while keeping memory under control.

entries.title: Parallel and Distributed Exploration
entries.heading: Parallel and Distributed Exploration
entries.text: Modern multi-core processors and distributed environments make it possible to parallelize state-space exploration. Work-stealing: Pseudo notation: Distributed hash tables: States are partitioned across multiple machines and explored cooperatively over the network. These techniques make it possible to verify systems that were previously too large to handle.

entries.title: 8.3 Describing Properties with Temporal Logic
entries.heading: 8.3 Describing Properties with Temporal Logic
entries.text: 8.3 Describing Properties with Temporal Logic

entries.title: The Challenge of Expressing Dynamic Properties
entries.heading: The Challenge of Expressing Dynamic Properties
entries.text: The correctness of a software system cannot be judged from a single snapshot. What matters is how the system behaves over time. Properties such as "every request is eventually answered," "deadlock never occurs," and "resources are distributed fairly" all concern dynamic behavior. Temporal logic is the language used to describe such behavior precisely. Ordinary logic talks about the current state. Temporal logic talks about how states evolve over time.

entries.title: CTL: The World of Computation Trees
entries.heading: CTL: The World of Computation Trees
entries.text: CTL (Computation Tree Logic) is one of the most widely used logics in model checking. It treats execution as a computation tree: each node is a state, each edge is a transition, and the branching structure represents alternative futures. Pseudo notation: CTL formulas combine path quantifiers with temporal operators. Path quantifiers: A (All paths): for all execution paths E (Exists path): for at least one execution path Temporal operators: X (neXt): in the next state F (Future): at some point in the future G (Globally): always U (Until): until

entries.title: Practical CTL Examples and Their Intuition
entries.heading: Practical CTL Examples and Their Intuition
entries.text: Safety: Pseudo notation: "Along all execution paths, an error never occurs." Liveness: Pseudo notation: "Along all execution paths, whenever a request exists, a response eventually occurs." Reachability: Pseudo notation: "There exists an execution path on which the goal is eventually reached." Fairness-oriented intuition: Pseudo notation: "At every point, there remains a future in which both process 1 and process 2 can eventually be scheduled."

entries.title: LTL: The View of Linear Time
entries.heading: LTL: The View of Linear Time
entries.text: LTL (Linear Temporal Logic) takes a different perspective. It treats execution as a single line of time rather than a branching tree. Basic LTL operators: ○ (Next): at the next moment ◇ (Eventually): eventually □ (Always): always U (Until): until Examples of LTL formulas: Pseudo notation:

entries.title: Differences in Expressive Power Between CTL and LTL
entries.heading: Differences in Expressive Power Between CTL and LTL
entries.text: CTL and LTL are suited to different classes of properties. A property that CTL expresses well: Pseudo notation: "It is always the case that there exists a path to a state in which reset is possible." This cannot be expressed directly in LTL. A property that LTL expresses well: Pseudo notation: "Once process 1 is granted access, process 2 is not granted access until process 1 releases it." This kind of ordering-oriented mutual exclusion property is much more natural in LTL than in CTL.

entries.title: Practical Property Patterns
entries.heading: Practical Property Patterns
entries.text: Several property patterns appear again and again in real verification tasks. Mutual exclusion: Pseudo notation: "The two processes are never in their critical sections at the same time." Deadlock freedom: Pseudo notation: "From every reachable state, there is at least one next state." Starvation freedom: Pseudo notation: "A waiting process is eventually served." Ordering constraints: Pseudo notation: "After event a, event b must not occur until event c occurs."

entries.title: Hierarchical Structure of Properties
entries.heading: Hierarchical Structure of Properties
entries.text: Complex systems benefit from organizing properties hierarchically. Level 1: Basic state validity: Pseudo notation: "The system is always in a valid state." Level 2: Protocol correctness: Pseudo notation: "Every sent message is eventually acknowledged." Level 3: Performance-oriented properties: Pseudo notation: "A response arrives within at most n time units."

entries.title: Negated Properties and Counterexamples
